steps.n: Forms a noun meaning 'that which is known/distinguished'. In Arabic, 'world' comes from the idea of everything that can be known or marked out.
Creates regular masculine plural form to show multiple worlds
Adds definite article prefix. This word is possessed by رَبِّ (Lord of), showing these are all the worlds that belong to Allah.
The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) to show it's a long vowel. Also adds a final fatḥa to show this word is the object of إِنِّى رَسُولٌ مِّن رَّبِّ (I am a messenger from the Lord of).

steps.m: to be true, to be right

steps.n: Root letters ح-ق-ق became حَقِيق by adding vowels and ي to make it mean 'someone/something that is true'
The tanwin shows this is describing Musa (Moses) in this ayah. He is saying he is obligated to speak only truth about Allah.
This is a preposition that shows something is on top of or required. Here it connects the word حَقِيقٌ (proper/worthy) to what comes after, showing what is proper/worthy.
Added an alif that shows the word ends with a long 'aa' sound. This doesn't change the meaning.
Added a small alif (ٓ) above to show the sound is stretched slightly when reading. This is just for pronunciation and doesn't change the meaning.
This is a particle that introduces what is worthy/proper to be done. Here it connects to حَقِيقٌ (worthy/proper) to show what is proper for Musa to do.
This is the basic negative particle used to negate things
Adding shadda makes the negation stronger and more emphatic
The alif is elongated to آ to show a longer pronunciation, but this doesn't change the meaning
Root letters ق-و-ل get vowels ُُ (double damma) to make 'he says' in present tense
Adding أَ prefix shows first person singular ('I')
Adding fatha at end shows this is in subjunctive mood

This is a preposition that shows something is on top of or about something else. In this ayah, it connects the word حَقِيقٌ (proper/worthy) to what follows, showing what is proper/worthy for the speaker to do.
Added shadda to show emphasis on the ل
Added kasra at the end to show possession or connection, and changed alif to wasl-alif at start
This is a basic exception particle that connects to the word ٱلْحَقَّ (the truth) that follows it. In this ayah, it's used to emphasize that the speaker will only say the truth about Allah.

steps.m: to be true, real, or right

steps.n: The shadda (ّ) doubles the middle letter to show this is a strong, definite truth
Adding ال makes it specific - this is THE truth, not just any truth. This word is what is being said (object of أَقُولَ meaning 'I say')
The fatha (َ) at the end shows this is what is being spoken - it's the object of 'I say' (أَقُولَ)

steps.m: indeed/certainly (emphasizing جِئْتُكُم - I have come to you)
steps.n: This is a particle that adds emphasis to the verb that follows it. Since it comes before the past tense verb جِئْتُكُم (I have come to you), it means the action definitely happened.

steps.m: to arrive/come to someone or something

steps.n: Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changed vowels to prepare for adding endings. This change happens because ي and ء are special letters that often change form.
Added تُ to show 'I' did this action in the past
Added كُم to show this action was done to or for multiple people being spoken to
Added vowels to make it past tense 'he made clear'
Added shadda to intensify meaning
Added feminine ending to make it a noun meaning 'proof'

steps.m: with a clear proof (object of جِئْتُكُم)
steps.n: Added بِ meaning 'with' and tanwin kasra showing indefiniteness. This word is connected to the verb 'I have come to you' (جِئْتُكُم) in the ayah.
Basic preposition showing where something comes from or starts
The shadda (ّ) makes the word stronger, like saying 'directly from' or 'truly from'. This emphasis happens because the word comes after لَّا in the ayah.
Added basic vowels to connect the letters
The middle letter ب gets doubled with shadda, making it stronger
The word changed to show it belongs to or is connected to something (like 'my Lord' or 'Lord of')
The suffix كُمْ is a possessive pronoun meaning 'your' (plural masculine)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Changes from 'he sent' to 'send!' by adding أ at start - makes it a command

steps.m: so send! (command to Pharaoh to let the Children of Israel go)
steps.n: The فَ shows this command follows from the previous statement about bringing clear evidence
This is the basic preposition meaning 'with'
The final vowel changes from fatha to kasra to prepare for connecting with the pronoun
Added the first person singular pronoun ي with a fatha to mean 'me'

steps.m: to build or establish a family line

steps.n: Adding basic vowels to make it a past tense verb
This is a different word - not a transformation from 'he built'. It means 'children/descendants' and the ى and ٓ show it connects to 'Israel' after it
First part of the compound name, derived from the concept of serving or striving
The alif is written with a special mark (alif maqsurah with madd) to show elongation
Added the second part 'il' meaning Allah/God, making the complete name mean 'servant of Allah'. This was the name given to Prophet Jacob (يعقوب).
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past. This word is connected to the conditional statement that follows it ("if you have...")
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a hollow verb (has a weak middle letter). This is a common change that makes the word easier to say.

steps.m: if (introducing the condition 'if you have brought a sign')
steps.n: This is a conditional particle that introduces a condition. In this ayah, it connects two parts: the condition (having brought a sign) and the result (bring it forth).

steps.m: to exist or be in a state

steps.n: Added vowels (fatha) to make it past tense 'he' form
The middle letter و is removed and vowels change to make a command form - like saying 'Be!'
Added تَ to show 'you' (masculine singular) did this action in the past. This is part of Pharaoh speaking to Moses, challenging him to show a sign if he was truthful.
Connected the root letters and added vowels to make the past tense form
Added تَ at the end to show 'you' (masculine singular) did the action

steps.m: to show evidence or make clear
to show evidence or make clear
steps.n: Basic vowel marks (fatha) added above letters to show how to pronounce them
Changes the root 'to show' into a single 'sign/miracle' by adding a long aa sound and ة at the end
Adds tanwin kasra to show this is indefinite and being acted upon
Adds the preposition بِ meaning 'with'. This word is connected to جِئْتَ (you came) in the ayah, showing what was brought.
Changed to command form by adding sukun on first letter and kasra at end. The ي drops because it's a weak letter at the end of a command.
Added فَ at beginning to show this command comes after something else. In this ayah, it comes after Musa being asked to show a sign if he has one.
Combined the preposition ب with the pronoun ها. This refers back to the آيَة (sign) mentioned earlier in the ayah.
The alif is written with a long mark (آ) instead of regular alif (ا). This is just a writing style and doesn't change the meaning.

This is a conditional particle that introduces a condition - in this ayah, it connects 'if you brought a sign' with 'then bring it'. The first part (with إِن) is the condition, and the second part (with فَأْتِ) is what should happen if the condition is true.
Command form (imperative) - telling something 'to be!' by adding damma and sukun marks
Added تَ at the end to show this is talking about 'you' (masculine singular) in the past. In this ayah, this is addressing Prophet Musa/Moses.
This is the basic form of the preposition that shows something comes from somewhere or belongs to something
Added fatha at the end to connect smoothly to the next word الصَّادِقِينَ (the truthful ones). This shows مِنَ is linking to that word.
Connected the root letters with fatha to form the basic past tense verb
Added alif after first letter to show who does the action (like adding -er in English)
Added ين to make it refer to multiple truthful people (plural)
Adding the definite article ال makes the word definite ('the truthful ones' instead of just 'truthful ones')
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb

steps.m: he threw/cast (acts on عَصَاهُ - his staff)
steps.n: Added أ (alif) at the start to make it mean 'he made something be thrown' - here he made his staff be thrown down
Adds conjunction prefix to connect this action with previous events
Added the superscript alif (ىٰ) which is a spelling variation for words ending in ى
Added fathas and the weak letter و changed to ا at the end to make it a noun
Added هُ to show the staff belongs to him. This staff is what was thrown in the phrase 'he threw his staff'
This is a particle that shows something unexpected happened

steps.m: and then suddenly (connects to فَأَلْقَىٰ - 'and he threw')
steps.n: Adding فَ shows this happened right after he threw his staff. The فَ connects these two sudden events together in sequence.
This is a basic pronoun that refers back to something feminine mentioned before it. Here it refers to عَصَا (the staff) that was thrown.

steps.m: to slither or move in a flowing manner

steps.n: Root means 'to slither' - added special pattern and ان at end to make it mean 'snake' (something that slithers a lot)
Adding tanween (ٌ) shows this is any snake, not a specific one. This snake appeared when Musa's staff transformed.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added مُ at start and changed vowels to show something that does the action of making clear
Added shadda to emphasize the meaning

steps.m: a clear proof (describing عَصَاهُ - his staff)
steps.n: Added tanween (double damma) at the end to show this is an adjective describing 'his staff' as being clear/obvious
Added fatha marks to connect the letters and show this happened in the past. This word is connected to يَدَهُۥ (his hand) which shows what he pulled out.
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this action to what happened before it, showing this was another thing that happened in sequence

steps.m: to extend, reach out with hand/power

steps.n: The root letters form the noun 'hand' - a special short noun that drops its final letter. Not a verb transformation.
Added fatha at end to show this is the object being acted upon by نَزَعَ (he drew out)
Added هُ to show possession (his). The ۥ mark shows the vowel is lengthened. This word is what Musa drew out/pulled out.
This is a special word that shows something surprising or unexpected is about to happen

steps.m: and behold! (connects to him pulling out his hand)
steps.n: The فَ shows this happens right after Moses pulled out his hand (يَدَهُ). It connects these two events in sequence.
This is a pronoun used to refer back to something feminine that was just mentioned. In this ayah, it refers to يَد (hand) which was mentioned right before it.
Connected the root letters together with basic vowel marks
This form makes the word feminine and is commonly used for colors when describing feminine things
The ُ shows this word is the subject after فَإِذَا (and behold) in the phrase 'and behold, it was white'

steps.m: to look at or observe carefully

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the root letters together
Changed the word to show who does the action
Added ين to make it plural (more than two)
Added ال to make it definite, with shadda on the ن
Added لِ at the beginning to show 'for/to' and َ at the end to complete the word

steps.m: he said (subject of إِنَّ هٰذَا لَسٰحِرٌ عَلِيمٌ)
steps.n: Added fatha marks to make the basic past tense form. This word is connected to what comes after it - it introduces what the chiefs said about Moses.
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in hollow verbs

steps.m: to gather, to assemble in a group

steps.n: Adding fatha vowels creates basic past tense verb

steps.m: the noble assembly (subject of قَالَ - said)
steps.n: Adding ال makes it refer to a specific noble council or assembly. This group is doing the action of saying (قَالَ)

steps.m: the noble assembly (subject of قَالَ - said)
steps.n: The ُ shows this assembly is doing the action of saying
This is a preposition that shows where something comes from or what group something belongs to. Here it shows that 'the chiefs' are from/of 'the people of Pharaoh'
The kasra (ِ) under the م shows this word connects to what comes after it
Changes verb root into a noun meaning 'a group that stands together'
Adds kasra at end to show this word is being possessed by something
This is a proper noun for the ruler of ancient Egypt
The fatha (َ) at the end shows this word belongs to or is connected to the word قَوْمِ (people) before it, making the phrase mean 'people of Pharaoh'
Adding shadda changes this from a conditional word to an emphasis word. In this ayah, it emphasizes the chiefs' statement about the magician being knowledgeable.
Basic demonstrative pronoun that points to something nearby (masculine singular)

steps.m: this (pointing to لَسٰحِرٌ - a skilled magician)
steps.n: Added ه with alif above it (هٰ) at the beginning and fatha with alif (َا) at the end. The هٰ makes it clearer that we're pointing to something, while the َا helps with pronunciation. In this ayah, it's pointing to someone who the speakers are calling a skilled magician.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed the word to show who does the magic instead of the action itself
Added لَ at the beginning to add emphasis, like saying 'definitely' or 'surely'
Added tanween damma at the end to show this is the predicate (what's being said about the subject) after the word إِنَّ (indeed/verily)
Adding vowel marks to make it a past tense verb meaning 'to know'
Adding ي between second and third root letters makes this describe someone who has this quality very strongly
The double-damma at the end shows this is describing a quality in general terms, often used for divine attributes
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two fathas

steps.m: he wants/seeks (subject of يُخْرِجَكُم - he expels you)
steps.n: Added يُ at start to show present tense, changed middle vowels to match present form, and added ُ at end to show he is doing the action. This word is connected to يُخْرِجَكُم, showing what 'he wants' to do.

steps.m: that (connects يُرِيدُ 'he wants' to يُخْرِجَكُم 'expel you')
steps.n: This particle connects two verbs to show that one action leads to another. Here it connects 'he wants' to 'expel you' to show what Pharaoh wants to do.
Adding the sukun and kasra pattern changes the meaning from just 'exit' to 'make something/someone exit'
Adding يُ at the start shows 'he' is doing the action. This word is what Pharaoh 'wants' (يُرِيدُ) to do.
Adding كُم at the end shows who is being made to exit - 'you all'. In this ayah, Pharaoh is speaking to his people about Moses wanting to make them all leave their land.
This is a basic particle that shows something moving away from somewhere or coming from a source. In this ayah, it connects with أَرْضِكُمْ (your land) to show what they're being taken from.
The shadda (ّ) adds emphasis to the pronunciation but doesn't change the basic meaning. This emphasis often happens when certain letters come before مِن.
Connected the letters together and added basic vowel marks to make it readable
Added a small 'i' sound at the end to show the land belongs to someone
Adding كُمْ at the end to show 'your (plural)' - like saying 'the land belongs to all of you'
Starting with the question word ما which asks 'what'
Adding ذا makes the question more specific and emphatic, like asking 'what exactly?'

steps.m: so what (do you command)? (connected to تَأْمُرُونَ)
steps.n: Adding فَ shows this question is a result of what was said before. It connects to the verb تَأْمُرُونَ (you command) that comes after it.

steps.m: to command/order someone to do something

steps.n: Added short 'a' vowels (fat-ha marks) to make it 'he commanded' in the past tense
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Changed from 'he' to 'you' by replacing يَ with تَ at the start
Added ونَ to show that multiple people are doing the action
Adding vowel marks to make basic past tense verb
The root قَوَلَ changed to قَال and وا was added at the end to show multiple people (they) did the action
Added optional marks (alif khanjariyah and small high rounded zero) that don't change the meaning
Added vowel marks (fatha) between the root letters to make 'he' form of past tense verb meaning 'delayed'
Changed from 'he delayed' to 'postpone!' by making it a command. The أ at start and vowel changes show it's a command. The last weak letter is dropped.
Added ه with sukun to show the command is being done to 'him' (Moses). This appears in a conversation where Pharaoh's chiefs are suggesting what to do about Moses.
The root letters أ-خ-و are joined with the vowel 'a' (fatha) to make the simple word for 'brother'. The و at the end drops off.
Added alif at the end to show this is the object of the verb أَرْجِهْ (postpone/delay)
Added هُ at the end to show possession - 'his brother'

steps.m: and his brother (as object of أَرْجِهْ - postpone)
steps.n: Added و at beginning to connect this word to what came before it
Adding أَ at start makes it a command to someone
Adding sukun at end shows it's a firm command
Adds conjunction 'and' to connect this command with previous statements in the verse
This is a basic preposition that shows something is inside or within something else. Here it shows the messengers are being sent into the cities.
The ي at the end changes to ى when it's at the end of a word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't change the meaning.
Added vowels and ة to make the singular form for 'city'
The word for 'city' changes its middle letters to make it mean 'many cities' - this is a special plural form
Adding 'al' at the start makes it mean 'the specific cities' instead of just any cities
The kasra ending shows this word is being used as the object of a preposition

steps.m: to gather or collect things/people together

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Changed the word to show who does the gathering instead of the act of gathering
Added ين to show multiple gatherers. This word is what is being sent (the object) in the phrase 'send gatherers'
Changed the alif to an alif with overbar (آ) which is just a writing variation
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the past tense verb
The يَ at the start changes it from past (came) to present/future (comes/is coming)

steps.m: they come to you (object of سٰحِرٍ عَلِيمٍ - skilled magician)
steps.n: Changed from 'he comes' to 'they come' (و added), and كَ added at end to mean 'to you'. The magicians are the ones doing the coming.
The doubled middle letter makes the meaning stronger and more complete
Added بِ at the beginning to show this word describes how something is done (in this case, bringing every skilled magician)

steps.m: with every (of سٰحِرٍ عَلِيمٍ - skilled magician)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end because it's connected to the word that follows it (سٰحِرٍ meaning 'magician'). This shows that every magician should be brought.

steps.m: to do magic or create illusions

steps.n: Added alif after first letter and vowels to show who does the magic, like how adding '-er' to 'teach' makes 'teacher'
Added tanwin kasra (ٍ) at the end to show this describes 'every' (كُلِّ) in the phrase 'every magician'. Also changed the alif to a long alif (ٰ) which is just a writing style variation.
Added vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from a past-tense verb (he knew) to a special pattern with ي that means 'someone who really knows a lot'

steps.m: a very knowledgeable one (describing سٰحِرٍ - magician)
steps.n: Added tanwin kasra to show this describes the magician mentioned in the ayah
Basic meaning of coming or arriving
Added fatha marks and connected letters to make past tense verb. The middle letter ي becomes ا because it's a weak letter.
Added long alif to emphasize the action of coming
Added و with fatha at start to mean 'and'. This word is the subject (doer) of the verb قَالُوٓا۟ (they said) in this ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make it a past-tense verb
Added shadda to show the magic is done with intensity or as a profession
Added alif after the shadda and ة at the end to make it mean 'one who does magic professionally'
Added 'the' (ٱل) at beginning and changed ending to show multiple magicians who are doing the speaking
This is a proper noun, the title of ancient Egyptian rulers

steps.m: Pharaoh (as the object of جَاءَ - they came to)
steps.n: The fatḥa at the end shows Pharaoh is being acted upon - the magicians came to him
Basic meaning of speaking or saying something
Added vowels to connect the root letters and show past tense
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a hollow verb
Added وا to show multiple people (they) did the action
Added optional pronunciation marks (elongation on و and small و above) that don't change the meaning
This is an emphasis particle that makes what comes after it stronger or more certain. Here it makes what they said more emphatic, like saying 'they said INDEED' instead of just 'they said.'
Basic pronoun meaning 'us' or 'we'
Added ل at the beginning to show this is about receiving something (in this case, a reward). The magicians are asking Pharaoh if there will be a reward FOR them.
Basic meaning of giving payment or reward
The root letters أجر become a noun meaning 'a reward' with tanween (the -an sound) showing it's indefinite. It's receiving the action in the sentence after إِنَّ (indeed).
The لَ adds emphasis because it comes after إِنَّ (indeed/certainly) in the ayah, making the statement stronger
This is a word that shows a condition - it connects two ideas where one depends on the other. Here it introduces the condition for receiving the reward (أَجْرًا) mentioned later.
Basic meaning of being or existing
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he was' to 'they (feminine) were'
Added نا at the end to show 'we' did the action
This is the basic form of the pronoun meaning 'we'
Added vowel marks to show this is being used as the subject of a sentence. The ُ (damma) at the end shows it's doing the action.
Basic meaning of defeating or winning
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added alif after first letter and kasra under second letter to show who does the action
Added ين to make it plural (more than one person). The ين ending shows it's being acted upon in the sentence

steps.m: the victorious ones (predicate of كُنَّا/we were)
steps.n: Added 'the' (ال) at the start to make it specific - like changing from 'victorious ones' to 'THE victorious ones'. The long alif (ٰ) is just written differently but sounds the same.
Changed from basic root 'say' to 'he said' by adding vowel marks. This is the past tense verb that introduces what someone said - like quotation marks in English.
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in Arabic verbs with و in the middle. This is still the past tense form.

steps.m: yes (in response to قَالَ - he said)
steps.n: This is a simple word used to say 'yes' or agree with something. In this ayah, it's being used to answer what was said before (قَالَ - he said).
This is an emphasis word that makes what comes after it stronger, like saying 'truly' or 'definitely'
Added و at the start to connect this with what came before (نَعَمْ - yes)

steps.m: and indeed you all (referring to the magicians being addressed)
steps.n: Added كُمْ at the end to show this is talking to multiple people (the magicians)
This is a preposition that shows something comes from somewhere or belongs to a group. Here it connects to 'the close ones' (ٱلْمُقَرَّبِينَ)
Added َ (fatha) at the end to help connect smoothly to the next word
The لَ at the start adds emphasis, like saying 'definitely' or 'surely'
Doubling the middle letter makes it mean 'to cause something to be near'
Adding مُ at the start makes it mean 'the one who' and the ِ ending shows they receive the action of being brought close

steps.m: the one who is drawn near
steps.n: Adding ال makes the word definite

steps.m: the ones who are drawn near (plural)
steps.n: Adding ين shows we're talking about multiple people who are drawn near
Added vowels to connect the letters and show past tense
Changed middle weak letter و to ا, which is common for hollow verbs
Added وا at the end to show many people are doing the action. In this ayah, these are the magicians speaking to Prophet Musa.
Starting with the proper name Moses, which has no root since it's a borrowed name
Added يٰ at the beginning to show Moses is being called or addressed directly
Added vowel marks (ُ and َ) and elongation marks (ٰٓ) to help with pronunciation
This is the basic conditional particle that starts the word
The ن combines with م to make a stronger م (shown by ّ), changing the meaning to show a choice between options

steps.m: either/whether (introducing first option before أَن تُلْقِىَ)
steps.n: The alif is written long to show it's pronounced clearly. This word introduces the first of two options, followed by another إِمَّآ for the second option.
This is a connecting word that joins 'O Moses' with what they want him to do (throw). It works like 'that' in English when you say 'they want that he goes'
Changed root letters into past tense 'he' form by adding a-vowels (fatha) between letters
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Changed from 'he' to 'you' by replacing يَ with تُ
Added a mark at the end to show it's connected to 'or' - either Musa throws first or the magicians throw
The alif is elongated to show emphasis and help with pronunciation

steps.m: and either/whether (connecting this choice with what came before)
steps.n: The و connects this option with the previous part of the sentence, showing these are connected choices
This word connects two ideas, showing what comes next is the main point. Here it connects 'they said' to what they actually said about throwing.
Connected root letters with fatha to make basic past tense verb
Changed first vowel to damma and removed middle vowel to make command form
Added نَ at beginning to show 'we' as the subject
Added shadda on ن because it comes after أَن in the ayah
This is the basic form of the separate pronoun meaning 'we'
Added damma (ُ) at the end to show this is the subject doing the action in نَّكُونَ (we will be)
Adding vowel marks to show this is a past tense verb
Added مُ at start to make it mean 'one who' and changed the word pattern to show the doer of the action
Added ين ending to show this refers to multiple people
Added ال to make it specific - these particular throwers speaking to Moses
Changed the root letters ق-و-ل into the past tense 'he said' by adding the vowels. This shows Pharaoh speaking to his magicians.
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in hollow verbs (verbs with و or ي in middle)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form basic past tense verb
Added أ at beginning to show intentional or forceful throwing
Changed to a command for a group by adding وا at the end (like saying 'you all throw!' instead of 'he threw')
This is a temporal word that shows when something happens, like saying 'at the time when' in English
Added فَ at the beginning to connect this with what happened before, showing one thing follows another
The alif at the end is written with a long آ mark to show proper pronunciation, but doesn't change the meaning
Added basic past tense vowels. The final ي changes to ى because it's a weak letter at the end.
Added أ at beginning to show causing someone else to do the action
Added وا at the end to show many people did the action. The ى changes to و because it's followed by وا.

steps.m: to do magic, to cast spells

steps.n: Added the vowels 'a' to make it mean 'he did magic' in the past
Adding plural suffix وا with connecting damma. This word describes what the magicians did after they threw down their ropes and staffs.
Adding the silent alif (۟) after وا to show it's a plural past tense verb
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to make the basic noun for 'eye'
Changes 'eye' to its plural form for small numbers (3-10 eyes)
Added fatha at the end to show this word is being acted upon by the verb سَحَرُوا (they bewitched)

steps.m: to be social or familiar with others

steps.n: Changes from verb 'to be social' into noun meaning 'people'
Added ٱلْ at the beginning to make it specific, like saying 'the people' instead of just 'people'
Added a shadda to emphasize the ن, making the word stronger
Added kasra at the end because this word belongs to أَعْيُنَ (eyes) in the phrase 'eyes of the people'
Connected the root letters with basic past tense vowels
Added است pattern to show attempting or seeking to do the action

steps.m: and he tried to cause fear
steps.n: Added 'and' (وَ) at the start to connect with what came before

steps.m: and they tried to cause fear
steps.n: Changed from 'he' to 'they' by adding و (waw) at the end

steps.m: and they tried to terrify them

steps.n: Adding basic past tense vowel pattern with alif for the weak middle letter
Adding damma ending to show this is in the nominative case
Changed the spelling pattern to make it a noun instead of an action. This is like changing 'to cook' to 'cooking'

steps.m: with magic (describing how they did something in أَلْقَوْا)
steps.n: Added بِ at the start to show they did something using magic, and added tanwin at the end to show it's any magic (not a specific magic). This word explains how they performed the action mentioned in أَلْقَوْا (they cast)
Added vowels (fatha-damma-fatha) to make the root into a past tense verb meaning 'became great'
Changed the word from a verb (action) to an adjective (description)
Added the tanween mark to show this describes the word سِحْرٍ (magic) in the ayah
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Added أ at start to show causing someone else to do the action
Added نا to show 'we' did the action
Added و at start to connect this with what came before it
This is a basic preposition showing direction or destination. Added kasra (ِ) under the first letter to show how it's pronounced.
Added the alif maksura (ىٰ) at the end with a small alif above it. This is the standard way to write this preposition when it appears before a word. Here it connects to مُوسَىٰٓ (Moses).

This is a proper noun of non-Arabic origin (borrowed from Hebrew/Ancient Egyptian). It doesn't follow typical Arabic root patterns. The name possibly comes from Egyptian meaning 'born of' or 'child of', though some suggest it means 'drawn out of water' in Hebrew.
Added damma (ُ) on م and fatha (َ) on س to show correct pronunciation
Added alif maksura with superscript alif (ىٰ) to show proper elongation of the final sound

steps.m: Moses (the prophet, who is being addressed)
steps.n: Added small alif (ٓ) to show this is direct speech addressing Moses
This is a basic connecting word (particle) that links two parts of a sentence. Here it connects 'we inspired' to what comes after it.
Added the vowel marks to show exactly how to pronounce it - أَ (a) at the start and نْ (n with silence mark) at the end
Added vowels to connect the letters and make a past tense verb
Added أ at start to show making something happen

steps.m: throw! (a command from Allah to Moses)
steps.n: Changed the word to a command form since Allah is telling Moses what to do

steps.m: to use a stick or staff

steps.n: Root letters ع-ص-و became the noun 'staff' by adding vowel marks and changing و to ا at the end
Added كَ to show the staff belongs to you. This word is what Moses is commanded to throw in the phrase أَلْقِ عَصَاكَ (throw your staff).
This is a conditional/temporal particle that shows something happens right after something else, or shows surprise
Added فَ at the beginning to show this happens right after what came before. This word connects to تَلْقَفُ (it snatches) to show when the snatching happens.
Basic form of the feminine pronoun. In this ayah, it refers back to Moses's staff (عَصَا) which is feminine in Arabic.
Added vowel marks to show proper pronunciation: kasra (ِ) under ه and fatha (َ) after ي
Changed the regular ي to ى (alif maqsurah) which is just a writing variation with the same pronunciation

steps.m: to catch or swallow something quickly

steps.n: Basic past tense verb showing someone did this action

steps.m: it (feminine) catches/swallows quickly (subject: عَصَاكَ - your staff)
steps.n: Changed to present tense feminine form since the subject is 'staff' (عَصَاكَ) which is feminine in Arabic. The staff is actively swallowing the magicians' tricks.
This is a relative pronoun referring to the things the magicians were fabricating. In this context, it's being used as the object of the verb تَلْقَفُ (it swallows).
Added fatha mark to show the correct pronunciation.
Added vowel marks to connect the root letters and show this happened in the past
Adding يَ at start and changing vowels shows this is happening now or will happen. The ْ (sukun) on the أ helps with pronunciation.

steps.m: they turn away from truth (referring to the magicians' deceptions)
steps.n: Adding ونَ shows multiple people are doing this action. In this ayah, it refers to the magicians and their deceptive tricks.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to show this happened in the past. This word is describing what happened to الْحَقُّ (the truth) in this ayah.
Added فَ at the start to show this happened as a result of what came before it in the story - when the truth became clear, it fell into place
The doubled letter shows this is a strong, established truth or reality
The ُ shows this word is the subject doing the action. Here, it's what 'came to pass' or 'became manifest' in the verse فَوَقَعَ (then it came to pass).
Adding ال makes it refer to the specific truth/reality being discussed, not just any truth. The ُ continues to show it's what 'came to pass'.

steps.m: to be useless, invalid, or false
it became false/invalid (result of فَوَقَعَ ٱلْحَقُّ - the truth became manifest)
steps.n: Added fatha marks to show this happened in the past. This describes how the magicians' tricks became false after the truth was shown.

steps.m: and it became false/invalid (connected to فَوَقَعَ - then fell/became manifest)
steps.n: The وَ shows this happened as a result of the truth becoming manifest - their magic became false after the truth was shown

steps.m: that which (refers to what was falsified in وَبَطَلَ)
steps.n: This is a special word that connects what comes before with what comes after. Here it refers back to the false magic that was proven wrong.

steps.m: to be or exist (in a state/condition)

steps.n: Added fatha marks to connect the letters and show past tense. This word is connected to يَعْمَلُونَ (they do/work) in this ayah.
Adding وا changes 'he' to 'they' (masculine plural). The small mark after وا shows the end of the word. This group is doing the action in يَعْمَلُونَ (they do/work).
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
The يَ at the start shows this is happening now

steps.m: they work/act (referring to what they were doing)
steps.n: The ون ending shows this is about multiple people doing the action. In this ayah, it refers back to what the magicians were doing.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Changed vowels to show this happened to someone instead of someone doing it
Added وا at the end to change from 'he' to 'they' (plural)

steps.m: so they were defeated (connected to وَٱنقَلَبُوا۟)
steps.n: Added فَ to show this happened as a result of something before it. This connects to وَٱنقَلَبُوا۟ (and they turned back) in the ayah.
This is the basic location word meaning 'here', used to point to a place close to the speaker
Adding لِ changes the location from 'here' (close) to 'there' (far away)
Adding كَ makes the location more specific, like pointing to an exact spot
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
The ان at the beginning shows the person did this action to themselves or it happened automatically
The وا suffix changes the subject from singular 'he' to plural 'they', making it masculine plural past tense

steps.m: and they turned back/returned (after being defeated)
steps.n: Adding وَ connects this to the previous phrase فَغُلِبُوا۟ (they were defeated) - they were defeated and then turned back
The root letters got vowels added to make a past tense verb meaning 'he became small/humbled'. It shows something that happened, not just a description.
The verb 'became small' changes to 'one who is small/humbled' by using the active participle pattern. The ين ending shows it's plural, describing multiple people who are in this humbled state.
Adding ين makes it plural, describing multiple people who are humbled

steps.m: those who are humbled (in accusative case)
steps.n: The َ (fatha) at the end shows this word is in the accusative case
Connected the root letters with fatha marks. The ي changes to ى because it's at the end of the word.
Changed to passive voice to show the action was done to something/someone rather than by someone

steps.m: and was thrown (connected to سٰجِدِينَ - prostrating)
steps.n: Added و to connect this word with what comes before it. This word describes how the magicians fell into prostration.
Added ة to make it refer to people who do magic as their role
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the س with shadda because it comes after ال

steps.m: the magicians (subject of أُلْقِىَ - were thrown/fell)
steps.n: Added damma to show this is the subject of the verb أُلْقِىَ (were thrown/fell) in this ayah
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) called an overbar. This is just a writing variation and doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: those who are prostrating (describes how السَّحَرَةُ 'the magicians' fell down)
steps.n: Makes the word plural to show multiple magicians prostrating. This describes how the magicians fell down in submission.
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and show past tense
The و changes to ا because it's between two fathas
Added وا to show multiple people did the action. This is what they said before saying 'We have believed'
Added optional marks (elongation sign on و and small high rounded zero) that don't change the meaning
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this is something that happened in the past
Added an alif at the start to show causing trust/safety in something = believing in it

steps.m: we believed (subject of قَالُوٓا۟ - they said)
steps.n: Added نَّا at the end to show 'we' are doing the believing. This word is what the magicians said after seeing the truth.

steps.m: to care for, nurture, develop something to its full potential
lord, master (one who nurtures and sustains)
steps.n: The root letters combine into a noun with a shadda, turning the meaning from 'to nurture' into 'one who nurtures'

steps.m: by/with the Lord (object of ءَامَنَّا - we believed)
steps.n: Adding بِ at the start shows this is how or by what means they believed. The kasra at the end shows it's connected to the next word (ٱلْعٰلَمِينَ)
Added basic vowels to form a noun meaning something that makes things known or distinguishable
Adding alif creates a noun meaning something vast or encompassing
Added ين to make the word plural, showing multiple worlds or realms

steps.m: the worlds/realms (referring to all created realms, as mentioned in relation to رَبِّ/Lord)
steps.n: Added ٱلْ to make it definite and final vowel َ. The word relates to رَبِّ (Lord) in the phrase رَبِّ ٱلْعٰلَمِينَ (Lord of the worlds)

steps.m: Lord (one who nurtures and cares completely)
steps.n: Added fatha and doubled the middle letter to create a noun meaning 'one who does this action perfectly'
The kasra shows this word is connected to what comes after it (Moses and Aaron). It's like saying 'Lord of' instead of just 'Lord'
This is a proper name borrowed from Hebrew/Egyptian. It doesn't come from an Arabic root. In this ayah, Moses is mentioned alongside Aaron.
This is a proper name borrowed from Hebrew. It doesn't come from an Arabic root.
The وَ at the start means 'and', connecting Aaron's name to Moses who was mentioned just before. The َ at the end shows this is the object of the sentence.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create past tense. This word refers to what Pharaoh (فِرْعَوْنُ) said.
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in hollow verbs
Added vowel marks to show how this foreign word is pronounced in Arabic. This is a proper noun borrowed from ancient Egyptian language meaning the ruler of Egypt.
Added damma (ُ) at the end to show this is the subject doing the action in the sentence. Pharaoh is the one who 'said' (قَالَ).
Adding alif makes it mean 'to actively show trust/faith'
Changed to second person plural past tense. This is Pharaoh speaking to the magicians who believed in Moses.
This is a basic preposition that shows how something is done or where it happens

steps.m: with/in him/it (referring to belief/faith in this ayah)
steps.n: Added the pronoun هِ to show who/what the preposition relates to. The ۦ is just a pronunciation mark showing the vowel extends.
Basic noun form showing time relationship. In this ayah, it means 'before I give permission' - Pharaoh is angry that people believed before he allowed it.
Adding fatha at end shows this word is being used as an object in the sentence

steps.m: that (introduces what Pharaoh is saying about ءَاذَنَ)
steps.n: This is a particle that connects two parts of speech. Here it introduces what Pharaoh is saying about giving permission, connecting 'before' with 'I gave permission'

steps.m: I gave permission (in response to فِرْعَوْنُ)
steps.n: Changed from basic 'allow' to 'I allowed' by adding hamza with fatha (ءَ) and alif to show Pharaoh speaking about himself. The hamza and alif together (ءَا) make it a question, like saying 'Did I give permission?'
Added fatha to show the basic preposition meaning

steps.m: for/to you all (object of ءَاذَنَ - permit)
steps.n: Added the pronoun كُمْ to show who the preposition is directed to. This word is connected to ءَاذَنَ (permit) in the ayah, showing who wasn't given permission.

steps.m: indeed/certainly (emphasizes هٰذَا لَمَكْرٌ - 'this is a plot')
steps.n: This is one of the most common emphasis words in Arabic. The shadda (ّ) makes the word stronger, changing it from a basic word meaning 'if/that' to a word that strongly emphasizes what comes after it. In this ayah, Pharaoh is using it to strongly emphasize his accusation that the believers are plotting against him.
This is a demonstrative pronoun that points to something near. In this ayah, it's pointing to the plot/scheme (مَكْرٌ) that Pharaoh is accusing the believers of making.
Changed the root into a noun. This word follows إِنَّ هٰذَا (indeed this) in the ayah, telling us what 'this' refers to.

steps.m: surely a plot/scheme (predicate of إِنَّ هٰذَا)
steps.n: Added لَ at the beginning to add emphasis, showing Pharaoh's strong accusation against the believers
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make basic past tense verb
Added تُمُ to show multiple people being addressed (you all)
Added و to show something was plotted (the object)
Added هُ to show what was plotted (referring back to the plan/scheme mentioned)
Adding shadda makes the action more intense/emphatic
This is a preposition that connects to the word الْمَدِينَةِ (the city) to show where something happens. Together they mean 'in the city'.
Added vowel marks to connect the root letters
Makes a noun for a place where people settle
Adds definite article to make the noun definite

steps.m: of the city (in فِى ٱلْمَدِينَةِ - in the city)
steps.n: Adding kasra shows this word follows فِى (in/within)

steps.m: to leave or make something/someone leave

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Added alif at beginning to show causing someone else to do the action
Changed to present tense by adding تُ at start and changing vowels
Added لِ to show purpose - this is happening in order to achieve something

steps.m: so that you all make (something/someone) leave
steps.n: Added وا to show multiple people are doing the action
The آ is an orthographic variant of ا at word end

steps.m: to inhabit, settle, be familiar with

steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this word is being acted upon by the verb تُخْرِجُوا (to expel/drive out)
Added هَا at the end to show these are the people of it/her (referring to الْمَدِينَةِ - the city). This whole word is what is being driven out according to the verb تُخْرِجُوا.
Added vowel marks to show this is a future particle that turns present actions into future ones
Added فَ at the beginning to show this happens as a result of something before it. This connects to the verb تَعْلَمُونَ (you will know) that comes after it.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
The ت at the start shows this is happening now and the person doing it is 'you'
Adding ون at the end makes it plural - now talking to many people instead of one
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make it a past tense verb
Adding shadda doubles the middle letter ط to show intense or repeated cutting
Changed to 'I' form by adding أُ at start and changing vowels - still means intense cutting

steps.m: I will DEFINITELY cut off (with emphasis)
steps.n: Adding نَّ at the end makes the verb super emphatic - like saying 'I will DEFINITELY do it!'

steps.m: I will surely, definitely cut off
steps.n: The لَ at start shows this is a strong promise or oath

steps.m: hand (object of أُقَطِّعَنَّ - I will cut)
steps.n: The root letters form the word 'hand'. In this sentence, someone's hand is getting cut off (it's the thing receiving the action of cutting).
The word changes from singular 'hand' to plural 'hands' by adding أ at the start

steps.m: your hands (object of أُقَطِّعَنَّ - I will cut)
steps.n: Adding 'your' (كُمْ) to make it 'your hands'

steps.m: foot, leg (not the action of walking)

steps.n: Added vowels to make the basic singular noun form
The word for 'foot' becomes plural by adding 'a' at the start and changing the middle vowel - like how we say 'foot' to 'feet' in English

steps.m: your feet/legs (object of لَأُقَطِّعَنَّ - I will surely cut off)
steps.n: Added كُم to show possession by multiple people. Added فتحة before كم because this word is the object of the verb لَأُقَطِّعَنَّ (I will surely cut off)

steps.m: and your feet/legs (coordinated with أَيْدِيَكُمْ - your hands)
steps.n: Added و meaning 'and' to connect this with the previous word أَيْدِيَكُمْ (your hands)
Added كم meaning 'your (plural)' to show who the feet/legs belong to
This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. Here it connects to خِلٰفٍ (opposite sides) to show how the cutting will be done.
Added sukun (ْ) at the end to show this is a complete word that connects to what follows.
Added shadda (ّ) to emphasize the م sound because it follows another م in speech.

steps.m: to be opposite or different from something

steps.n: Added kasra and alif to make a noun meaning 'opposite sides'. This word follows مِنْ (from) in the ayah, showing which direction the hands and feet will be cut from.
Added tanween kasra (ٍ) at the end to show this is any opposite sides in general, not specific ones. Also changed the alif to an alif with overbar (ٰ) which is just a writing variation.
Basic form of the connecting word that shows one thing happens after another with some time in between
Added marks (ُ and ّ) to show this means 'and then' - it connects two actions in order
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Doubling the middle letter makes the meaning more intense and specific
Changed to 'I' form by adding أُ at start, shows something I will do
Added لَ at start to mean 'surely/definitely' I will crucify
Added نَّ for extra emphasis

steps.m: I will most definitely crucify you all
steps.n: Added كُمْ to show the action is done to multiple people
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb

steps.m: he caused to gather/he gathered together
steps.n: Adding أ at the beginning changes it from 'gather' to 'cause to gather'
Changed from a verb (to gather) to a word meaning 'all together' by adding ين, which makes it describe multiple people
Added basic past tense pattern with fatha marks
Changed to common past tense form with alif
Added وا at the end to show multiple people did the action. This word is connected to what follows, where they say 'Indeed we...'
Added optional marks: elongation sign (ـٓ) and small high rounded zero (۟) which don't change the meaning
This is a special word that adds emphasis to what comes after it, like saying 'truly' or 'definitely' at the start of a sentence
Added آ at the end to show 'we' are doing the action. This word connects to مُنقَلِبُونَ (returning) to say 'indeed we are returning'
This is a basic preposition that shows direction or destination. In this ayah, it connects to رَبِّنَا (our Lord) to show where they are returning to.
Added the small alif (ٰ) above the ى to show this is the full form of the word. This doesn't change the meaning, it just helps with proper reading.
Added fatha on first letter and doubled the second letter to show strength
Added نَا at the end to show it belongs to us. The kasra (ِ) comes before نَا because the word follows إِلَىٰ (to) in the ayah.
Added vowels (a) to make it 'he turned' in the past tense
Added reflexive pattern to show the action happens to the subject
Changed to show the person who returns/is transformed (like changing 'returned' to 'returner')

steps.m: those who are returning (to رَبِّنَا - our Lord)
steps.n: Added masculine plural ending to show multiple people. In this ayah, it refers to the magicians speaking about their return to their Lord.
Basic interrogative particle meaning 'what'. In this ayah, it's asking what they are criticizing.
Added و at the beginning to connect this question with what came before it
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb

Added تَ at start to show 'you' are doing it, changed middle vowel to kasra and added damma at end to show it's happening now. This is addressing Pharaoh who is resenting the believers for their faith.
This is a preposition that shows something coming from somewhere or someone
Added نا at the end to show this is happening to 'us'. The ن from مِن combines with the ن from نا using a shadda

steps.m: from us (in response to وَمَا تَنقِمُ - and you do not resent)
steps.n: The final alif is elongated because this word comes at the end of a phrase, showing a natural pause in speech. This word is responding to the previous phrase 'and you do not resent'

steps.m: except (connecting to أَنْ ءَامَنَّا - that we believed)
steps.n: This is a frozen form particle that isn't derived from a root. It connects what comes before with what comes after, showing an exception. Here it introduces the reason why they are taking revenge - only because the believers believed in Allah's signs.

steps.m: except (connecting to أَنْ ءَامَنَّا - that we believed)
steps.n: The regular alif (ا) is written as a long alif (آ) in the Quran. This is just a writing style and doesn't change the meaning or pronunciation.
This is a connecting word that joins two ideas together. Here it connects 'you blame us' with 'we believed', showing that our belief is the reason for the blame.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb
Added alif after hamza to change meaning from basic trust to religious belief
Doubling the last letter makes the meaning stronger and more emphatic
Added نا to show we are the ones who believed
The root letters combine to make 'sign' by stretching the first letter into آ and adding ة at the end
Changes to plural form by replacing ة with ات
Adds kasra to show this word is connected to what comes after it (رَبِّنَا - our Lord)
Adds بِ at beginning to mean 'by/with' and changes spelling slightly while keeping same pronunciation

steps.m: to care for, nurture, and develop something to its full potential

steps.n: Connecting the letters with fatha and doubling the middle letter shows this is someone who nurtures intensely and perfectly
The kasra at the end shows this word is connected to something that comes after it

steps.m: Our Lord (the one being called upon)
steps.n: Adding نَا at the end shows we are talking about our Lord. In this ayah, it's used as a way of calling out to Allah.
This is the basic negation particle that refers to past actions
Adding a shadda (double letter) to م turns 'not' into 'when' - like turning 'did not' into 'at the time when'

steps.m: when (referring to جَآءَتْنَا - they came to us)
steps.n: Adding ا at the end makes it specifically refer to a completed past action. In this ayah, it refers to when the signs came to them.
The root letters changed to make 'he came' by adding vowels and turning ي into ا
Extended alif adds emphasis to the action
Added ت with sukun to show feminine/neutral subject
Added نا at the end to show the action affected 'us'
Adding fatha marks creates basic past tense verb
The root رَبَبَ becomes رَبّ (Lord), then adding نَا means 'our', and the long آ at the end shows we're calling out to Him - like saying 'O our Lord!'
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb
Adding أ at start makes it mean 'to make something pour out' or 'to empty something into another'
The ى changes to ي with sukun when something is attached after it. This is just a spelling change that doesn't affect the meaning.
Added نَا at the end to show this means 'us'. This word connects to أَفْرِغْ (pour forth) in the ayah, showing what should be poured upon.
Changed the root into a noun by adding vowel marks. This makes it name the quality of being patient rather than the action.
Added ًا to show this word is receiving the action of 'pouring/bestowing' - the believers are asking Allah to pour patience upon them
Added vowels (َ) to make it past tense 'he took fully'
Intensification of meaning through doubling middle root letter
Adding تَ creates reflexive/passive meaning from Form II base
Adding conjunction prefix و meaning 'and'
Adding نَا at the end makes this word mean 'us' as the object of the verb. In this context, it's a request to Allah to take their souls in death.

steps.m: to submit, surrender, be at peace

steps.n: The مُ prefix and ِ on the middle letter work together to show who does the action of submitting. This is a special pattern used for people who do actions.
The ين suffix makes this a regular masculine plural noun

steps.m: Muslims (as the ones being given patience in توفنا)
steps.n: The َ at the end shows this word is receiving the action of being taken (in death) from the verb توفنا in the ayah
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create basic past tense form
The middle letter و becomes ا - this is a common change in past tense verbs to make them easier to say
Added و with fatha at beginning to connect this statement with what came before. This word starts a new sentence where the chiefs (ٱلْمَلَأُ) are speaking.

steps.m: to gather/assemble (especially as nobles or leaders)

steps.n: Adding the basic vowel marks makes it a past tense verb
Adding ال makes it refer to a specific group of nobles/chiefs being discussed
The ُ shows this group is doing the action of speaking (قَالَ) in this verse

This is a helper word that connects two nouns to show that the first one (قَوْمِ meaning 'people') comes from or belongs to the second one (فِرْعَوْنَ meaning 'Pharaoh'). Together they mean 'people of Pharaoh'.
The root meaning of standing together becomes a word for a group of people who stand as one. This is a common way Arabic creates words for groups.
The ِ ending shows this word belongs to or is connected to Pharaoh (فرعون) who comes right after it in the ayah. This is like saying "Pharaoh's people" in English.
This is a proper noun borrowed from ancient Egyptian language meaning 'great house' that became the title for Egyptian kings

steps.m: Pharaoh (as the object of قَالَ الْمَلَأُ - the chiefs said)
steps.n: The fatha ending shows this word is being acted upon - here, Pharaoh is being addressed by the chiefs in his court
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense verb
Changed from 'he left' to 'you leave' by: 1) adding تَ at start to mean 'you', 2) changing vowels to show present time
Added أَ at the start to make it a question. This word is part of what the chiefs said to Pharaoh about Moses.

steps.m: Moses (the prophet being addressed by Pharaoh's people)
steps.n: This is a proper noun of Hebrew origin (מֹשֶׁה/Moshe) meaning 'drawn out' (referring to being drawn from water). The long alif (ىٰ) at the end shows it's a foreign name adapted into Arabic.

steps.m: to stand together, rise up as one

steps.n: The root meaning of standing together becomes a noun for a group that stands as one unit
The word قَوْم (people) gets هُ (his) attached to it and becomes قَوْمَهُ (his people). The fatha shows it's being acted upon by the verb أَتَذَرُ (will you leave)
The و connects this word to what came before it
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a past tense verb
Changes from 'he did' in the past to 'he does' in the present by adding يُ at the start
Changes from singular 'he corrupts' to plural 'they corrupt'

steps.m: so that they spoil/ruin (purpose of أَتَذَرُ - 'will you leave')
steps.n: Adds لِ at beginning to show why something happens. In this ayah, it's connected to the question 'will you leave' - they're asking Pharaoh if he will leave Moses and his people to cause trouble.

steps.m: in (connects ٱلْأَرْضِ to the verb لِيُفْسِدُوا۟)
steps.n: This is a preposition that shows location. Here it connects 'the earth' (ٱلْأَرْضِ) to the verb 'to spread corruption' (لِيُفْسِدُوا۟), showing where the corruption would happen.
Added short vowels (fat-ha and sukoon) to make it readable as 'ard' meaning earth/land
Added ال to specify we're talking about THE earth, not just any land

steps.m: in the earth (location in فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ)
steps.n: Added kasra because it comes after the word في (in/on) in the phrase فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ (in the earth)
Added يَ at beginning to show present tense action and ُ at end to show it's happening directly

steps.m: he leaves you alone (object of أَتَذَرُ - 'will you let')
steps.n: Added كَ at end to show the action is happening to 'you'. This connects to the earlier question أَتَذَرُ (will you let) in the verse.
Added وَ (and) at start to connect this with the previous phrase about Moses and his people

steps.m: to worship or take as a deity

steps.n: Adding ت at the end makes this word specifically refer to female gods/deities
Adds possession - 'your gods'. This word is the direct object (accusative case) of the verb يَذَرَكَ meaning 'to leave/abandon you', so the full meaning is 'abandon you and your gods'.
Adds و meaning 'and' to connect this to the previous phrase about 'leaving you'
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past

The middle letter و changes to ا because it comes between two fathas. This makes the word easier to say. This word is connected to سَنُقَتِّلُ (we will kill) which comes after it, showing what was said.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Adding shadda makes the action stronger or repeated
Changed to present tense 'we' form by adding نُ at start, changing middle vowel to kasra, and adding ُ at end
Added سَ at beginning to show this will happen in the future
Forms the singular noun 'ibn' (son) from root related to building/establishing
Changes from 'son' to 'sons' by breaking up the word pattern - a common way to make plurals in Arabic

steps.m: their sons (object of سَنُقَتِّلُ - we will kill)
steps.n: Adding هُمْ shows possession (their). The word is the object of the verb سَنُقَتِّلُ (we will kill) in this ayah.
Adding vowel marks to make it a past tense verb
Added pattern that shows seeking or wanting to do something
Changed from past tense 'he' to present tense 'we' by adding نَ at the start

steps.m: and we spare life (of نِسَآءَهُمْ their women)
steps.n: Added 'wa' (and) at start, same word meaning 'to keep alive/spare' but now specifically refers to sparing the women mentioned next
Changes root letters to make plural form meaning 'women' - common pattern for groups

steps.m: women (object of سَنُقَتِّلُ - we will kill)
steps.n: The fatha shows this word is receiving the action of killing mentioned earlier in the verse

steps.m: their women (object of سَنُقَتِّلُ - we will kill)
steps.n: Adding هم shows these are the women belonging to the people being discussed. This word receives the action of killing mentioned earlier.
This is a word that adds emphasis to what comes after it, like adding 'definitely' or 'truly' in English
Added ا at the end to show this is about 'we'
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this with what came before it, like saying 'and'

steps.m: to be above or over something

steps.n: Changed from basic root meaning 'above' into a word that shows where something is located (above/over something else)

steps.m: above them (referring to the Children of Israel)
steps.n: Added هُمْ which refers back to the Children of Israel who Pharaoh is claiming power over
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the past tense verb
Changes the verb into a noun describing someone who does the action
Adds damma to show this is the subject of the sentence

steps.m: ones who overpower (plural, subject of إِنَّا)
steps.n: Changes from single to plural by adding ون, showing multiple people who overpower
Added basic past tense pattern with fatha on each letter. This word refers to what Musa (Moses) said.
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in hollow verbs
This is a proper noun borrowed from Hebrew/Egyptian meaning 'Moses'. In this ayah, Moses is the one who is speaking (he is the subject of قَالَ - 'he said') to his people.
Changed the root meaning from an action (standing) to a group of people who stand together
The word gets a kasra (-i) sound at the end because it comes after a word that needs this change
Added لِ at the beginning to show this is talking about doing something to or for these people

steps.m: to his people (who Moses spoke to)
steps.n: Added هِ at the end to show these are Moses's people that he is speaking to
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Added است at the beginning to show seeking or requesting help
Changed from past tense 'he' to present tense 'I' form, and the middle letter و changes to ي in present tense
Added وا to make it a command for a group
This is the unique proper name of God in Islam. It comes from the root meaning of divinity but is treated as a distinct proper noun.
Added shadda on ل to show it is doubled in pronunciation

steps.m: by/with Allah (object of اسْتَعِينُوا - seek help)
steps.n: Added بِ (meaning 'by/with') at the start and a kasra ( ِ ) at the end to show Allah is the one we seek help from. The ٱ helps connect the sounds smoothly.

steps.m: to be patient or endure hardship

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changes from 'he was patient' to 'be patient!' by adding اِ at start and changing the ending - this makes it a command to one person
Changes command from singular to plural by adding وا

steps.m: and all of you be patient!
steps.n: Adds و meaning 'and' at the start and changes spelling of alif to match standard Quran writing

This is a special word that adds emphasis. When Musa (Moses) speaks to his people, he uses this word to strongly emphasize what he's about to say, like starting a sentence with 'Listen!' or 'Know that...' in English. It makes the next word change its ending.

steps.m: earth, ground, or land (as a basic concept)

steps.n: Basic root letters got vowel marks to make the word 'earth/land'
Added 'the' to make it specific

steps.m: the earth (as the object being inherited)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this is the object being inherited in the phrase 'يُورِثُهَا' (He causes to inherit it)

steps.m: belongs to Allah (connected to الأرض/the earth)
steps.n: Added لِ at the beginning to show ownership/belonging and ِ at the end to connect it to the previous word الأرض (the earth). This shows that the earth belongs to Allah.
Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb meaning 'inherited'
Added alif at start to show causing someone else to inherit
Changed from past to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels

steps.m: he makes her/it inherit (referring to الأرض/the earth)
steps.n: Added ها to show the earth (feminine) is what's being made to inherit
This is a basic word used to refer to people in a general way, like saying 'whoever' or 'anyone who' in English. In this ayah, it refers to whoever Allah wishes from among His servants.
Added fatha mark on م to show how to pronounce it correctly
Added fathas and the weak letter ا replaced ي because of pronunciation rules
Changes from past to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing the ending vowel to ُ
Extended the alif with آ for pronunciation. This word is the subject of يُورِثُهَا (he causes to inherit it) in this ayah.
This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. Here it connects 'servants' to what came before it.
Added kasra (ِ) under م and sukun (ْ) on ن to show how to pronounce it correctly
Changes from single servant to multiple servants
Added kasra to show possession is coming
Added هِۦ to show possession by Him (Allah)

steps.m: to come after, to result from

steps.n: Adding vowels turns the root into a complete past tense verb
Added alif after first letter and ة at end to create noun meaning 'outcome'
Added و with fatha at beginning to mean 'and'
Added ال (the) to make it 'the outcome' instead of just 'an outcome'
Added damma at end to show this is the subject of the sentence, and changed alif to long alif for writing style

steps.m: one who guards against evil/is mindful (of Allah)
steps.n: Forms a word for someone who does the action of protecting/guarding themselves against wrong by being mindful of Allah
Makes the word refer to multiple people who have this quality

steps.m: the ones who are mindful (of Allah)
steps.n: Adds the definite article to make the word definite

steps.m: for the ones who are mindful (of Allah) (العاقبة belongs to them)
steps.n: Adds لِ meaning 'for/to', showing that the good end (العاقبة) mentioned in the ayah belongs to these people
Added vowels and changed و to ا because it's a hollow verb (has و in middle). This makes it past tense.
Added وا at the end to show many people (they) did the action
Added optional marks (ٓ and ۟) that don't change the meaning but help with proper recitation
Changed to passive form to show something was done to someone
The نَا at the end means 'we' - shows who received the action
Changed from active to passive voice to show we received/were affected by the harm
This is a particle that shows where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it connects the word قَبْلِ (before) to أُوذِينَا (we were harmed), showing when the harm started happening.

steps.m: to come before or face something

steps.n: Added fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to create the word meaning 'before'

steps.m: before (connected to أَن تَأْتِيَنَا - 'that you come to us')
steps.n: Added kasra at the end because this word is connected to what comes after it in the sentence

steps.m: that (connects to تَأْتِيَنَا - you come to us)
steps.n: This is a connecting word that joins two ideas together. Here it connects the previous word with تَأْتِيَنَا (you come to us), showing what happened. It's like saying 'before that you came to us'.
Connected the root letters together
Added ت at beginning to show 'you' as the doer, with vowels to show present tense
Added نَا at the end to show 'us' as the receiver of the action. This verb follows أَن which makes it subjunctive (like 'to come' in English)
Basic preposition meaning 'from'

Added small alif (ٰ) to show elongation of the final sound
Added vertical fatḥa (ٓ) to show connection to the next word أَجَلٍ (appointed term)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create a noun meaning a specific period or deadline

steps.m: a deadline/term (that they were to reach)
steps.n: Added tanwin kasra to show this is indefinite and is being acted upon by something else in the sentence. In this case, it's the deadline they were supposed to reach.
This is a pronoun that shows who is doing the action. In this ayah, it connects to the verb يَنكُثُونَ (they break/violate), emphasizing that THEY are the ones doing the breaking.

steps.m: to reach, arrive at, or attain

steps.n: Added short vowels (fat-ha) to make it 'he reached' in past tense
Changed to active participle form showing who/what does the action
Makes the word plural for multiple people doing the reaching
Added هُ to show what is being reached. This word describes أَجَلٍ (appointed term) in the ayah.
This is the basic form of the word that indicates a point in time

steps.m: whenever (referring to the time when they break their promise)
steps.n: Adding ا makes it refer to repeated or conditional time. In this ayah, it refers to whenever they break their promise after the punishment is removed.

This is a standalone pronoun that means 'they' or 'them' when talking about a group of men or mixed group. In this ayah, it's connected to the word بٰلِغُوهُ (reaching it), showing who is doing the reaching.
Added يَ at the beginning to show the action is happening now or in the future, and added vowels to connect the letters
Added ُ at the end to show this is the main verb in the sentence
Added ون at the end to show multiple people (they) are doing the action

steps.m: to punish severely or take revenge

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make a past tense verb
Added اِنْتَ to make it mean doing the punishment/revenge for oneself (like getting back at someone)
Added فَ to show this happened as a result of previous events. The اِ changes to ٱ after فَ
Added نَا to show Allah speaking as 'We'
This is a preposition that shows something coming from somewhere or someone

steps.m: from them (referring to those who denied Our signs)
steps.n: Adding هُمْ shows this applies to 'them' - in this ayah referring to those who denied Allah's signs. This word connects to فَٱنتَقَمْنَا (so We took retribution) showing who the retribution was taken from.

steps.m: to drown or submerge in water

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Adding أ at the start changes it from 'to drown' to 'to make someone/something drown'

steps.m: and so he caused to drown
steps.n: Adding فَ shows this happened as a result of what came before (their denial of signs in this ayah)

steps.m: and so we caused to drown
steps.n: Adding نَا shows that 'we' did the action (referring to Allah using the royal 'we')
Adding هُمْ shows who received the action (them - referring to Pharaoh and his people)
This is a basic preposition showing location or containment. In this ayah, it shows that the drowning happened in the sea.
Added kasra (ِ) under ف to show how to pronounce it correctly as 'fee'
Changed the final ي to ى which is just a writing style variation - it means the same thing
Adding fatha makes it a past tense verb
The root يَمم (going towards water) becomes 'the sea' when you add ال (the). In this ayah it comes after the word فِى meaning 'in/into the sea'
The kasra shows this word follows the preposition فِى (in/into)
This is a basic particle that emphasizes what comes after it
Adding هُمْ refers to 'they' or 'them'
The prefix بِ changes the meaning to show reason or cause. This word explains why the punishment in the previous ayah happened.
The prefix ب changes the meaning from 'indeed they' to 'because they'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Doubling the middle letter shows they didn't just deny, but strongly rejected the truth. This word explains why Allah drowned them (فَأَغْرَقْنٰهُمْ)
Added plural masculine ending to show it was a group (Pharaoh and his people) who denied

steps.m: to show, appear, or be clear

steps.n: Changes from verb root to plural noun meaning 'clear signs'
Shows these signs belong to us (Allah)
Adding بِ shows these signs were used as tools or means. This word relates to كَذَّبُوا (they denied) in the ayah.
Added vowels to make the past tense form. The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a weak letter.
Added وا at the end to show multiple people did the action
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this word with what came before it. This word connects to كَذَّبُوا (they denied) in the ayah.
This is a preposition showing distance or being about something

steps.m: away from it (referring to آيٰتِنَا/Our signs)
steps.n: Added ها which refers back to آيٰتِنَا (Our signs) mentioned earlier in the ayah. This shows what they were being heedless about.

steps.m: to ignore or not pay attention

steps.n: Added short 'a' vowels (fatha) to make the past tense verb 'he ignored'
This form shows who does the action
Makes the word plural to mean multiple people who are heedless
Adding ال makes it definite (specific group) and fatha shows its grammar role
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Adding أ at the beginning changes the meaning from doing something yourself to making someone else do it
Adding نا shows that 'we' did the action instead of 'he'

steps.m: and we made (someone) inherit (subject of gave to الْقَوْمَ)
steps.n: Adding و connects this word to what came before it. This word is connected to الْقَوْمَ (the people) who received the inheritance.
Added fatha and sukoon to create the noun pattern فَعْل which turns the concept of 'standing firm' into 'a group that stands together'
Adding ال makes it specific

steps.m: the people (object of أَوْرَثْنَا - we caused to inherit)
steps.n: Adding fatha at the end shows this word is receiving the action of the verb أَوْرَثْنَا (we caused to inherit)
This is the basic form used to refer to someone or something
Added ين to make it refer to multiple people
Added ال to make it specific and definite
Added shadda on ل and fatha at the end. This word is receiving the action of 'We caused to inherit' (وَأَوْرَثْنَا) in this ayah.
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a hollow verb (has a weak middle letter). This is a common pattern in Arabic.
Adding وا at the end changes 'he was' to 'they were'. This word is connected to يُسْتَضْعَفُونَ (they were being made weak) in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a past tense verb
Added يُ at start to show present tense, and سْتَ in the middle to show someone is making them weak (not weak by themselves). Changed from 'being weak' to 'being made weak by others'.
Added ونَ to show this happens to multiple people

steps.m: to shine from the east (like the sun)

steps.n: Added مَ at the start to show where something happens, like how 'mosque' (مَسْجِد) means 'place of prostration'
Added ا after first root letter to make it plural, showing many places
Changed ا to ٰ (long alif) and added َ at the end to show this word connects to 'the earth' (الْأَرْضِ) in the ayah
Added vowel marks to show how to pronounce the word correctly
Added 'al' to make it specific and kasra at the end because it's connected to the word مَشٰرِقَ (eastern parts) in the ayah

steps.m: to go west, to set (like the sun)

steps.n: Root 'west' changed to show many places where the sun sets (plural noun)
Adding conjunction prefix to connect to previous phrase
Adding ها at the end shows it belongs to something feminine mentioned before (the earth/الأرض)
Base demonstrative pronoun for feminine singular. Written with ى instead of ي at the end.
Adding ٱل changes it from pointing to something (this/that) to connecting parts of a sentence (which/that)
Adding shadda emphasizes the connection. In this ayah, it refers back to the lands (مَشٰرِقَ ٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَغٰرِبَهَا) that Allah blessed.
Added alif with bar (ٰ) after first letter and sukun on last letter to prepare for suffix
Added نا at the end to show 'we' did the blessing. This relates to فِيهَا (in it) which refers to the land mentioned before.
This is a basic preposition that shows location or containment. It's one of the most common Arabic prepositions.

steps.m: in it (referring to الْأَرْضِ - the earth)
steps.n: Added ها which refers back to الْأَرْضِ (the earth) mentioned earlier in the ayah. The ها is feminine because الْأَرْضِ (earth) is feminine in Arabic.
Connected the root letters with vowels and doubled the middle letter م to show completeness. This verb is describing كَلِمَتُ (word) in the ayah.
Added ت at the end because كَلِمَتُ (word) is feminine in Arabic

steps.m: and she/it became complete (subject: كَلِمَتُ - word)
steps.n: Added و with fatha to connect this phrase to the previous part of the ayah about inheriting the land
Changes to past tense verb pattern with middle letter having kasra sound
Added ة to change from 'speaking' to mean 'one word' - like changing from an action to a thing
Added ُ to show this word is doing the action in the sentence - it's the subject of the verb تَمَّتْ (was completed)

steps.m: to care for, raise, nurture to completion

steps.n: The doubled middle letter (shadda) makes this a noun for someone who does the action perfectly and completely
Adding kasra shows this word belongs to or is possessed by another word (كَلِمَتُ - word/promise)
The word 'Lord' (رَبِّ) becomes 'your Lord' (رَبِّكَ) by adding كَ at the end, which means 'your'. This word belongs to كَلِمَتُ (word/promise) in the full phrase.
Root meaning 'good/beautiful' becomes a noun meaning 'goodness' by adding the vowels ُ and ْ
Added ى at the end to make it feminine and show it's the highest degree
Added ال to make it specific. This word describes كَلِمَتُ (word) in the ayah, showing that Allah's word/promise is the most beautiful and perfect.
This is a preposition that shows something is upon or over something else. In this ayah, it connects how Allah blessed (بٰرَكْنَا) the Children of Israel (بَنِىٓ إِسْرٰٓءِيلَ).
Added an alif symbol (ٰ) after the ى to show how to pronounce the word correctly. This doesn't change the meaning.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to form the basic verb
Changed from the verb 'to build' to a special form meaning 'children/descendants of' - commonly used in names like 'Children of Israel'
Added the long vowel mark to show this is plural form meaning 'children/sons'

steps.m: Israel (the prophet Jacob's other name, used here to refer to his descendants)
