steps.n: This is a proper name borrowed from Hebrew (יִשְׂרָאֵל/Yisrael) meaning 'one who struggles with God'. It refers to the prophet Jacob and his descendants. In this ayah, it appears as part of the phrase بَنِي إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ (Children of Israel).
This is a basic particle that shows how or why something happens

steps.m: with what/because of what (connects to صَبَرُوا - they were patient)
steps.n: Added مَا which means 'what' to show this refers to their patience mentioned in صَبَرُوا (they were patient). Together it means 'because of what' referring to their patience.
Basic meaning of root letters
Added fatha vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Added وا at the end to show multiple people did this action

steps.m: to destroy completely, to demolish utterly
steps.n: Adding the shadda (ّ) on the middle letter makes the destruction more complete and intense. Like the difference between 'break' and 'shatter' in English.
Adding نَا at the end shows that 'we' did the destroying. The ْ (sukun) is added before نَا to help pronunciation.

steps.m: and we utterly destroyed (what فِرْعَوْنُ used to make)
steps.n: The وَ (and) connects this to the previous statement. This destruction refers to what Pharaoh used to make/build, as mentioned in the next part of the ayah.
This is a connecting word that links to what comes after it. Here it connects to what Pharaoh and his people were doing/making.
Added vowels to connect the letters and show past tense

steps.m: he was (describing what فِرْعَوْنُ وَقَوْمُهُ used to do)
steps.n: The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a weak letter in the past tense. This word is used to describe what Pharaoh and his people used to do.

steps.m: to make or create something with skill

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make the past tense form
Changes past tense to present tense by adding يَ at start, putting sukun on first root letter, and adding ُ at the end. This word comes after كَانَ (was) in the ayah.
The words are the same - just written slightly differently (with/without a small mark)
This is a proper noun borrowed from ancient Egyptian 'per-aa' meaning 'great house'. It became the title for Egyptian kings.
Added vowel marks to show how the word is pronounced: فِ (fi) رْ (r) عَ (a) وْ (w) ن (n)
Added ُ (damma) at the end to show this is the subject doing the action in يَصْنَعُ (he makes/builds)

steps.m: to rise up, to stand firm, to establish

steps.n: The root meaning of standing/rising becomes a noun for a group that stands together as one community
The ُ shows this word is doing the action in the sentence

steps.m: his people (doing the action with فِرْعَوْنُ)
steps.n: Adds هُ meaning 'his' to show these are Pharaoh's people. This word joins فِرْعَوْنُ (Pharaoh) as a subject of يَصْنَعُ (makes/builds)

steps.m: and his people (doing the action with فِرْعَوْنُ)
steps.n: Adds conjunction meaning 'and'

steps.m: what/that which (refers to what فِرْعَوْنُ was building)
steps.n: This is a relative pronoun that refers back to what Pharaoh and his people were building

steps.m: and what/that which (refers to what فِرْعَوْنُ was building)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the letters
The و changes to ا because it's a hollow verb (has a weak middle letter)

steps.m: they were (subject of يُسْتَضْعَفُونَ - were being made weak)
steps.n: Added وا to show multiple people (they) did the action. This word connects to يُسْتَضْعَفُونَ showing who was being made weak.

steps.m: to build or construct (especially raised structures)

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels

steps.m: they build/construct (referring to Pharaoh and his people)
steps.n: Added ون to show multiple people doing the action. This refers to Pharaoh's people building their structures.
This root carries the basic meaning of crossing from one point to another
The root ج-و-ز becomes 'we made pass' by: 1) Adding alif (ا) after ج shows someone made the action happen 2) Adding نا at the end means 'we' did it
Adding و with fatha at the beginning connects this action to what came before it in the verse
Adding vowel marks to create basic past tense verb form
Added بِ (bi) at the start to mean 'with' or 'by'. Also, بَنِي here means 'children/descendants' not 'he built'
The word بِبَنِي is already complete for this context - no additional changes needed

steps.m: Israel (the name of Prophet Yaqub عليه السلام)
steps.n: This is a proper noun borrowed from Hebrew meaning 'one who struggles with God'. It was the name given to Prophet Yaqub (Jacob) عليه السلام.

steps.m: Israel (the name of Prophet Yaqub عليه السلام)
steps.n: Added proper Quranic spelling with diacritical marks. The hamza is written with a long alif (ٰٓ) in Quranic text.
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to create the noun meaning 'sea' - a vast body of water that splits through land
Added ال to specify 'the' specific sea, and َ at the end because it's being acted upon by the verb جٰوَزْنَا (we crossed)
Added vowels to make the basic past tense form
Changed ى to وْ to show multiple people did the action
Added ا after و to complete the plural form

steps.m: and so they came (after crossing the sea)
steps.n: Added فَ to show this happened after crossing the sea mentioned in the previous verse

This is a preposition that shows the people were focused on their idols. Since it's a preposition, it connects what comes before it (يَعْكُفُونَ - they devoted themselves) to what comes after it (أَصْنَامٍ - idols).
Added a small alif (ٰ) after the ى to show it's pronounced as a long 'aa' sound at the end. This doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: to stand firmly, rise up, establish

steps.n: Root meaning 'to stand' becomes a noun meaning 'people' - like how people stand together as a group. The word is in the genitive case after عَلَىٰ (upon).
Added tanween kasra (ٍ) at the end to show this is an indefinite noun coming after the word عَلَىٰ (upon).

steps.m: to devote oneself by staying in one place

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels

steps.m: they devote themselves (by staying at their idols)
steps.n: Added ون to show multiple people are doing the action. In this ayah, this refers to a group of people devoting themselves to their idols.
This is the basic form of the preposition that shows something is on top of or over something else. In this ayah, it connects to قَوْمٍ (people) to show the Children of Israel came upon some people.
Added alif maksura (ىٰ) at the end which is just a spelling variation - it doesn't change the meaning
Added a small alif (ٓ) above the word which helps with pronunciation but doesn't change the meaning

steps.m: to shape or form into an image/idol

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic noun meaning 'idol'
Added أ at beginning and ا after second root letter to make plural form. Changed middle vowel to sukun.
Added tanwin kasra at the end to show this is indefinite and is the object being worshipped by the people (قَوْمٍ) mentioned in the ayah
Starting with the basic particle ل which shows direction or purpose
The shadda (ّ) adds emphasis because this word comes after a word ending in ل, causing the two ل sounds to merge
Added هُ to show this is about 'him'
Added مْ to change 'him' to 'them', showing this applies to multiple people
Added fathas to make it past tense. The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two fathas - this is a common change in Arabic verbs that have و in the middle.
Added ُوا at the end to show many people are speaking. In this ayah, they are speaking to مُوسَى (Moses).
This is the proper name Moses (Musa in Arabic). It's a borrowed name that entered Arabic from ancient Egyptian through Hebrew.
Added يٰ (ya) at the beginning to call out to Moses directly. This is like saying 'Hey Moses!' or 'O Moses!' in English. The long alif (ٰ) shows this is being called out.
Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic past tense form
Changed from 'he made' to 'make!' by removing vowels - this is not actually the command form which would be اِجْعَلْ

steps.m: make! (command to Moses from his people)
steps.n: Added hamzat al-wasl at beginning to help pronunciation. This word is part of what Moses's people said to him, asking him to make them gods like the idolaters had.
Starting with the basic preposition that shows direction or purpose
Adding emphasis with shadda to show importance or certainty
Adding نا at the end to show this is about "us". The alif at the end (آ) is added to show the long vowel sound more clearly when stopping at this word.
Root letters أ-ل-ه became the word for 'god' by adding إِ at the start and vowel marks
Added a small alif (ٰ) after the second letter to make the 'aa' sound longer
Added tanween fatha to show this is the object of the verb اجْعَل (make). The people are asking Moses to make this for them.
Added fatha to the letter ك which is a particle meaning 'like' or 'similar to'

steps.m: just as/like (referring to how they have idols)
steps.n: Added ما which makes the comparison more general. This word connects to لَهُمْ ءَالِهَةٌ (they have gods), showing the similarity between what they're asking for and what the other people have.
Added fatha to the letter lam to show it's a preposition

steps.m: for them/belonging to them (referring to أَصْنَامٍ - idols)
steps.n: Added هم which means 'them' to show who the preposition is referring to. In this ayah, it refers back to the idols (أَصْنَامٍ) that some people were devoted to.
Transforms root into singular noun meaning 'deity'
Adds alif to make it a noun describing type of deity
Added tanween dammah to show this is any deity/god, not a specific one
Changes to broken plural form by adding آ at start
Adds tanween dammah to make the noun indefinite
Added fathas to make past tense base form. This shows the action happened in the past.
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a special type of word. This doesn't change the meaning.
Adding shadda changes this from a conditional 'if' to an emphatic 'indeed'

steps.m: indeed you all (addressing the Children of Israel)
steps.n: Added كُمْ at the end to show who is being addressed - in this case, the Children of Israel who are asking Moses to make them idols

steps.m: a group that stands together (after عَلَىٰ)
steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to create a noun meaning 'a group that stands together' - like how people who stand together form a community. This word follows the preposition عَلَىٰ (upon).
Added tanween damma (ٌ) at the end to show this is an indefinite noun (like saying 'a people' instead of 'the people')
Changed from root letters to past tense 'he' form

steps.m: you do not know/are unaware (addressing Moses)
steps.n: Changes to present tense and second person. In this ayah, this is addressing Prophet Moses.

steps.m: you all do not know/are unaware (addressing Moses's people)
steps.n: Added ون at the end to change from speaking to one person (Moses) to speaking to many people (Moses's people). Like changing 'you don't know' to 'you all don't know'.
Added kasra (ِ) under the first letter. This is the basic form of this emphasis word.

steps.m: indeed/truly/verily (strongly emphasizes هٰٓؤُلَآءِ مُتَبَّرٌ - 'these are destroyed')
steps.n: Added shadda (ّ) to make the emphasis stronger. This word introduces and emphasizes the statement that follows about those people being destroyed.
This is the basic pointing word that shows something is near, like saying 'this' in English
Adding ؤ makes it point to one specific thing or person
Adding لَآ changes it from pointing to one thing to pointing to many things
The ءِ at the end shows we are pointing directly at them, like saying 'Look at THESE people!'

steps.m: to break into pieces, destroy completely
something that is destroyed/broken into pieces
steps.n: Adding مُ at the start and doubling the middle letter (ب) shows this is talking about the thing that gets broken, not the one doing the breaking

steps.m: something that is destroyed/broken (referring to the false gods/idols they worship)
steps.n: Adding the ٌ mark shows we're talking about any destroyed thing in general, not a specific one. In this ayah, it refers to the false gods that the people worship.
This is the basic form of the word that connects parts of sentences by referring to something mentioned before

steps.m: what/that which (connecting وَبٰطِلٌ with كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ)
steps.n: The shadda (ّ) on م shows this word connects two parts of the sentence together - linking 'worthless' with 'what they used to do'

steps.m: they/them (referring to مُتَبَّرٌ - those who are destroyed)
steps.n: This is a pronoun used to refer back to the people mentioned earlier in the ayah. Here it refers to those who are being destroyed (مُتَبَّرٌ).
Shows location or position

steps.m: in it (referring back to what they are doing مَّا يَعْمَلُونَ)
steps.n: في (in) + هِ (it) = فيه (in it) - the هِ points back to something mentioned later
Changes from verb 'to be false' to noun meaning 'that which is false'
Adding double dammah makes it an indefinite noun - 'a false thing' rather than 'the false thing'

steps.m: and (it is) false/useless (describing مَّا هُمْ فِيهِ - what they are in)
steps.n: Added وَ at start to mean 'and', connecting it to the previous statement. This word describes 'what they are in' from the ayah.

steps.m: that which (connects to هُمْ فِيهِ - 'they are in it')
steps.n: This is a connecting word (particle) that doesn't come from a root. Here it connects what is being destroyed (مُتَبَّرٌ) with what these people are involved in.

steps.m: that which (connects to هُمْ فِيهِ - 'they are in it')
steps.n: The shadda (ّ) on م makes the sound stronger for emphasis, like saying 'indeed that which' or 'precisely what'
Adding the fatha mark makes this a past tense verb
Adding another fatha completes the basic past tense verb pattern
Adding وا makes it refer to a group of people (they). This word is connected to يَعْمَلُونَ (they do/work) in the ayah, showing what they were doing.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Adding ي at the start changes it from past (he worked) to present (he works/is working)
Adding ون makes it plural masculine

steps.m: he said (subject of أَغَيْرَ ٱللَّهِ أَبْغِيكُمْ)
steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to show this happened in the past. This word introduces what Musa said next.
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a hollow verb

steps.m: to be different from, other than

steps.n: Added vowel marks to the root letters to make a word meaning 'other than' or 'different from'

steps.m: other than (object of أَبْغِي - I seek)
steps.n: The fatha at the end shows this word is the object - the thing being sought in the question

steps.m: is it other than? (object of أَبْغِي - I seek)
steps.n: Adding أَ at the start turns this into a question
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters. This is a unique word in Arabic that always refers to the One True God.

Added kasra at the end to show possession, shadda on ل for emphasis, and changed initial alif to wasl form since it comes after another word. This word is possessed by أَغَيْرَ (other than) in the phrase 'other than Allah'.
Added vowels to connect the root letters and make a past tense verb
The prefix 'أ' with fatha indicates first person singular in present tense verbs

steps.m: I seek/want you all (as a deity for اللَّهِ)
steps.n: The suffix كُمْ shows that multiple people are being sought. In this ayah, Musa is asking his people if he should seek for them any deity other than Allah.

steps.m: to worship or take as a deity

steps.n: Changes from the action of worship into a noun meaning 'one who is worshipped'
The alif is written as a small mark above to show the long 'aa' sound

steps.m: a god (object of أَبْغِيكُمْ - I seek for you)
steps.n: Adding ًا (tanween) makes it 'a god' - the direct object that is being sought
This is a pronoun that means 'he' and is doing the action of preferring (فَضَّلَ) in this ayah
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this to the previous phrase قَالَ أَغَيْرَ ٱللَّهِ أَبْغِيكُمْ إِلٰهًا (He said: Should I seek for you a god other than Allah?)

steps.m: to prefer, to favor, to give extra to

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb
The doubled middle letter makes the meaning more intense and shows he did this action to someone else

steps.m: he greatly preferred/favored you all (object of أَبْغِيكُمْ - I seek for you)
steps.n: Added كُمْ at the end to show this was done to 'you all'. This word is connected to أَبْغِيكُمْ (I seek for you) in the ayah.
This is a preposition that shows something is above or superior to something else. In this ayah, it connects to 'the worlds' (ٱلْعٰلَمِينَ) to show Allah preferred them above all others.
Added alif after first letter and fatha marks to create a noun meaning 'world' - something that can be known or understood
Added ين to make it plural. This word follows على (upon/over) in the ayah, which requires the ين ending.

steps.m: the worlds (object of فَضَّلَكُمْ عَلَى - preferred you over)
steps.n: Added ٱلْ (the) at the beginning to make it specific. The long alif (ٰ) is used instead of ا after ع. This word completes the phrase 'preferred you over the worlds'.

steps.m: when (referring to a past event that أَنجَيْنٰكُم describes)
steps.n: This is a time particle that introduces a past event. Here it introduces when Allah saved the Children of Israel from Pharaoh's people.

steps.m: and when (connecting this event to previous events)
steps.n: The و connects this event to other favors Allah mentioned before this ayah
Connected the root letters with fatha marks. The final و changes to ا because it's a weak letter at the end.
Adding أَ at the beginning makes it mean 'to cause' something. The sukun (ْ) helps with pronunciation.
Adding نَا at the end shows 'we' did the action. The ى gets a sukun (ْ) before نَا.
Adding كُم shows the action was done to 'you all'. The alif in نَا becomes ٰ (long a) when كُم is added.
This is a primary preposition that shows origin, source or starting point. It has no root or derivational steps as it is a frozen form.
Added shadda for emphasis and to connect smoothly with previous word. This preposition shows who you were saved from (Pharaoh's people).
Changed from a root meaning 'belong to family' into a noun meaning 'family/clan' by using a special hamza (ء) and stretching the sound with a long alif
Added kasra at the end to show this word belongs to or is connected to Pharaoh (فِرْعَوْنَ) in this ayah
This is the basic form of the proper noun for the ruler of ancient Egypt. Added vowel marks to show pronunciation.

steps.m: Pharaoh (as the object of أَنجَيْنٰكُم/We saved you)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this word is being acted upon - it's the one the people were saved from in the phrase 'We saved you from Pharaoh'
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels to damma
Added ون to show multiple people are doing the action

steps.m: they impose upon you all (referring to the Children of Israel being afflicted by Pharaoh's people)
steps.n: Added كُمْ to show who is being affected by the action. This refers to the Children of Israel who were being afflicted by Pharaoh's people.
The root becomes a noun describing the quality of being bad
Added damma to make it into a noun meaning the concept of evil

steps.m: evil (object of يَسُومُونَكُمْ - they made you suffer)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this is receiving the action of the verb يَسُومُونَكُمْ (they made you suffer). The long alif (ٓ) is added for pronunciation.
Added vowels and alif to make it into a noun meaning the result of punishment
Added kasra at the end to show this punishment belongs to or is connected to something

steps.m: the punishment (سُوٓءَ ٱلْعَذَابِ means 'worst of the punishment')
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific. In this ayah, it's part of the phrase سُوٓءَ ٱلْعَذَابِ meaning 'worst of the punishment' that Pharaoh's people inflicted.
The shadda intensifies the meaning to 'kill repeatedly/massacre'
Connected the root letters with basic past tense vowels
The shadda on the middle letter makes the action more intense or repetitive
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels
Added ون to show multiple people doing the action. This word is part of what Pharaoh's people were doing to the Children of Israel.
The root becomes a plural noun meaning 'children' or 'descendants'
The root meaning 'to build' becomes a broken plural noun meaning 'sons' by adding أَ at the start and changing the middle of the word
Adding the َ at the end shows this word is the object of a verb
Adding كُمْ at the end shows the sons belong to multiple people being addressed
Adding ا makes the word longer to show it's plural
Connected the root letters together
Added يَسْتَ pattern to mean 'to keep/let something happen'. Here it means to let someone stay alive rather than kill them
Added و at beginning to mean 'and'

steps.m: and they spare life (referring to Pharaoh's people)
steps.n: Added ون to show multiple people doing the action. In this ayah, it refers to Pharaoh's people who were keeping the women alive while killing the sons.
Connected the root letters and added vowels to form the irregular plural base. This is a special form for 'women' that doesn't follow regular patterns.
Added a hamza and fatha at the end to make the complete word for 'women'

steps.m: your women (referring to the Children of Israel being addressed)
steps.n: Added the plural 'your' ending to show these women belonged to the people being addressed (the Children of Israel in this story about Pharaoh)
This is a preposition showing location or containment. In this ayah, it connects to the word ذٰلِكُم (that) to show what something is in/within.
Added conjunction و meaning 'and'
The basic demonstrative pronoun ذا gets an alif above it (ذٰ) which is just a spelling variation that doesn't change the meaning
Adding لِ changes the meaning from 'this' (near) to 'that' (far)
Adding كُم shows this is being said to multiple people (you all)
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Changed the weak letter و to ا and added ء to make it a noun meaning the act of testing
Added tanween dammah to show this is indefinite (like saying 'a test' instead of 'the test'). The word is describing something from Allah in this ayah.
Basic preposition showing where something starts or comes from
Added kasra under م to show it's a preposition
Added shadda on م because it comes after a word ending in a vowel (أَنجَيْنٰكُم). This is a pronunciation rule to make it flow better when reading.
The doubled letter makes it mean 'lord' or 'master'

steps.m: Lord (one who nurtures and cares for all)
steps.n: Added fatha and doubled the middle letter to create the noun meaning 'Lord'
Added kasra to show this word owns or possesses something
Added كم to show this belongs to multiple people being spoken to
The ر has a shaddah (doubled), but the meaning stays the same - 'your Lord' addressing multiple people
Connected the root letters with vowels to create the basic past tense verb
Changed the middle vowel and added ي to make it into an adjective that describes something as having this quality
Added tanwin damma to show this is describing the trial (بَلَآءٌ) mentioned in the ayah

steps.m: to promise or set an appointment

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Adding وا and نا changed it from 'he promised' to 'we made a mutual promise with each other'

steps.m: and we made a mutual promise
steps.n: Adding و at the beginning connects this action to what came before it
Base form of the proper name borrowed from Hebrew/Egyptian meaning 'drawn out of water'
Added damma (ُ) on م and fatha (َ) on س to show correct pronunciation
Added small alif (ٰ) after ى to show elongation of the final sound
Adding basic vowel marks to make the word readable
Adding alif makes it the standard form for the number three
Added ين at the end to make it mean 'thirty' (like English -ty in thirty, forty, etc.)
Added fatha at the end because this word is receiving the action of 'We promised' (وَوٰعَدْنَا)
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make it a noun
The ة shows we're talking about one specific night
The ً (tanween) at the end shows this is the object being counted - it's receiving the action of the number forty
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb
Adding أ at start changes meaning to 'made something complete' instead of just 'completed'
Added نا at the end to change from 'he completed' to 'we completed'. The نا shows that 'we' did the action.
Added ها (meaning 'it') at the end to show what was completed. ها is attached to the end of the verb to become its object.
Added و at beginning to mean 'and'
Basic meaning related to the number ten
Basic noun form for the number ten
Adds the preposition 'with/by' to the number
Adds the genitive case ending
Basic meaning of completion
Added vowels and doubled the middle letter م to make it mean 'it became complete' in the past
Added فَ to show this happened as a result of what came before it in the verse
Adding fatha to complete Form I past tense pattern

steps.m: to set or appoint a specific time

steps.n: Added مِي at the beginning to show this is a place or time when something happens. The و changes to ي because it comes between two vowels.
Added ُ at the end to show this is the subject of the sentence. This word is connected to رَبِّهِ (his Lord) in the ayah.
Added fatha on first letter and shadda on second letter to create the noun meaning 'lord' from the basic concept of nurturing/caring
Added a small line (kasra) under the last letter to show this word belongs to/is connected to ميقٰتُ (appointed time)
Added هِ with an elongation mark (ۦٓ) at the end to show this lord belongs to him (referring to Moses)
Added أ at the beginning and changed vowels to make it into the basic number 'four'
Added ين at the end to change 'four' into 'forty'. The word is being acted upon by the verb تَمَّ (completed) in this ayah, showing what was completed - forty nights.
Connected the root letters and added vowel marks to form the basic noun 'night'
Added ة to show we're talking about one specific night
Added ً (tanween) to show this is the object being counted as 'thirty nights' in the verse
Base Form I past tense verb pattern before weak letter changes
Added fatha vowels and changed middle و to ا to make past tense verb
Added و at beginning to connect this word to what comes before it. This word connects to مُوسَىٰ (Moses) who is doing the saying.

steps.m: Moses (the one being addressed by Allah)
steps.n: This is a proper noun (name) that was borrowed into Arabic from ancient Egyptian/Hebrew. It refers to Prophet Moses, who is being directly addressed in this verse. The long alif (ىٰ) at the end shows this is a foreign name adapted to Arabic.
Connected the letters and dropped the weak letter و
The kasra prepares the word to connect with 'my'
The ي makes it mean 'my brother'
Added هِ to show the brother belongs to Musa
Added لِ to show direction/purpose - Musa is speaking to his brother
This is a proper noun (name) that came into Arabic from Hebrew. It refers to Prophet Aaron, the brother of Moses who is mentioned in this verse.
Connected the root letters with fatha to form the basic past tense verb
Removed first vowel and changed middle vowel to 'u' to make it a command to one person
Added helper alif to make the command easier to pronounce

steps.m: succeed/replace me! (command from Musa to Harun)
steps.n: Added نِى at the end to show the command affects 'me' (Musa is telling Harun to take his place)
This is a preposition that shows location or being inside something. Here it shows Moses is asking Aaron to be in charge of his people while he's away.
Connected the root letters and added vowel marks to form the noun meaning 'people' - comes from the idea of people standing together as a group
Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to something after it
Added ى at the end to show these are specifically 'my people'. In this ayah, Moses is speaking about his people while giving instructions to Aaron.
Basic meaning of the root letters
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a past tense verb
Added أ at the start to show making someone/something do the action (making something right/correct)
Changed from 'he improved' to 'improve!' by changing the vowels to make it a command to one person

steps.m: and improve!/correct! (command to Moses about his people)
steps.n: Added و to connect this command with previous statements to Moses about leading his people
This is the basic negative particle that means 'not'

steps.m: and not (connected to ٱخْلُفْنِى - be my successor)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this negative command to the previous positive command. In this ayah, Moses is telling Aaron 'be my successor AND DO NOT follow the way of the corrupters'
Added vowels to make basic past tense verb
Added prefix تَ to show 'you' as the doer and changed end vowels for present tense
Added shadda on middle letter to show intensive following
Changed from 'you follow' to 'do not follow!' by removing the last vowel after لا (makes it a command to not do something)

steps.m: to make a path or way

steps.n: Adding ي and vowels creates the common noun meaning 'path'
Added fatha at the end to show this is the object (thing being followed) in the phrase 'do not follow the path'
Connected the root letters with fatha to form the basic past tense verb
Added مُ prefix and changed vowels to make it describe someone who does the action
Added ين to make it plural, showing multiple people who corrupt
Added ٱلْ to make it specific and َ at the end because it's the object of تَتَّبِعْ (follow) in the ayah
Added ا at the end to complete the word for 'when'. This word introduces a past event that was followed by another event.
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before it. In this ayah, it connects to the next word جَآءَ (came), forming 'and when (Moses) came'.
Added vowel marks (fathas) to make it a past tense verb meaning 'he came'
Changed to hollow verb pattern by adding alif after first letter
Extended the alif to show emphasis/intensity of coming

steps.m: Moses (the prophet who spoke with Allah)
steps.n: This is a proper name borrowed from Hebrew. In this ayah, Moses came to meet with Allah at the appointed time and place.

steps.m: Moses (the prophet who spoke with Allah)
steps.n: Added damma (ُ) on م and fatha (َ) on س to show the correct pronunciation of the name.

steps.m: Moses (the prophet who spoke with Allah)
steps.n: Added alif khanjariyah (ٰ) after ى to indicate the extended sound at the end of the name.

steps.m: to set or fix a time

steps.n: Added مِ at start and changed root pattern to make a word for the place or time when something happens. The و changes to ي because it comes after مِ.
The ل prefix here means 'for' or 'to'

steps.m: for our appointed time (that Moses came to)
steps.n: Added نا ('our') at the end to show it was our appointed time. The لِ at the start means 'for/to'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
The doubled middle letter shows this was special, direct speech
The ه suffix means 'him'. This is a verb with an attached pronoun, not a noun that would have case endings.

steps.m: and he spoke directly to him (referring to how Allah spoke to Moses)
steps.n: The و with fatha (وَ) at the start means 'and'. This describes Allah speaking directly to Moses at the appointed time.

steps.m: to care for, raise, nurture completely

steps.n: The doubled middle letter (shadda) makes this an intensive form, emphasizing the complete nature of the nurturing

steps.m: his Lord (the one who spoke to Moses)
steps.n: Adding the attached pronoun هُ with prolongation mark ۥ to indicate possession. This word is the subject (doer) of the verb كَلَّمَ (spoke/talked), showing who did the speaking.
Added fathas to make past tense form. This shows the action happened in the past.
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two fathas. This word shows Allah's response to Moses.

steps.m: to care for, raise, help grow

steps.n: The kasra at the end shows we're calling out to Allah. This is Musa (Moses) speaking directly to Allah.
This word can mean either 'O my Lord!' (when calling out) or 'my Lord' (when describing). The meaning changes based on how it's used in the sentence
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Adding alif at start changes it from 'he saw' to 'I make someone see/show' - like turning 'see' into 'show'
Changing to command form by removing final weak letter and changing middle vowel to kasra
Adding نِي at the end to show the command is being done to 'me'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changed vowels to make it a command form - sukun on first letter, damma on middle letter
Added أَ at beginning and sukun at end to complete the command form
This is a basic preposition showing direction or destination. Since it's a particle, we start with its basic form rather than a root.
Added كَ at the end to show this is directed to 'you' (one male). In this ayah, Allah is speaking to Prophet Musa.
These are the basic letters that give us the idea of saying or speaking
Added short 'a' vowels (fatha) to make it past tense - 'he said'
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two vowels. This happens often with words that have و in the middle

steps.m: will not (negates تَرَىٰنِى 'you will see me')
steps.n: This is a special word that makes something impossible in the future. Here it's used with تَرَىٰنِى (you will see me) to say 'you will not see me.' It's like a strong way of saying no about something that hasn't happened yet.
Added vowel marks (fatha) to make it mean 'he saw' in the past tense
Added تَ at beginning to show 'you' and changed the end because of the weak letter ي
Added alif maksura (ىٰ) at the end to show the long vowel sound
Added نِى at the end which means 'me' - showing who is being seen

steps.m: but (contrasting with لَن تَرَىٰنِى - 'you will not see me')
steps.n: This is a particle that connects ideas by showing contrast. The ِ at the end shows it's connecting to what follows.

steps.m: and but (contrasting with لَن تَرَىٰنِى - 'you will not see me')
steps.n: Added و at start to connect this with the previous statement, showing this is a continuation of the conversation
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the past tense verb
Changed vowels to make it a command form
Added helper alif at start to make it easier to pronounce the sukun on first letter
This is a preposition that shows direction. In this ayah, it connects the verb أَنظُرْ (I look) with what is being looked at.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic noun for mountain
Added i-sound (kasra) at the end to show this mountain is connected to another word تَجَلَّىٰ (manifested) in the sentence
Added ال to show this is referring to the specific mountain that Allah manifested His glory upon

steps.m: if (introduces condition about استَقَرَّ - settling/remaining firm)
steps.n: This is a conditional particle that introduces an 'if' statement. Here it connects to the next word استَقَرَّ (settled/remained firm), setting up the condition 'if it remains firm'

steps.m: then if (connects to previous statement about looking at mountain)
steps.n: Added فَ to connect this condition with the previous statement about looking at the mountain. It shows sequence - first look at the mountain, then if it remains firm...

steps.m: then if (connects to previous statement about looking at mountain)
steps.n: Changed the ending vowel to kasra (ِ) because it's followed by a word starting with a connecting hamza

steps.m: to settle or remain fixed in place

steps.n: Doubling the ر makes the meaning stronger - like settling very firmly

steps.m: it became firmly settled (referring to the mountain مَكَانَهُ)
steps.n: Added ٱسْتَ at the beginning to show the process of becoming settled. This word relates to the mountain (الجبل) staying in its place.
This is the basic concept these letters represent
Added مَ at beginning to create a noun meaning 'place where something happens'. The و changes to ا because it's between two vowels.
Added fatha on ك to complete the noun pattern
Added هُۥ at the end to show this is 'its place' referring to the mountain (الجبل) mentioned in the ayah
Added vowel marks to show exact pronunciation. This word shows that something will definitely happen in the future.
Added فَ at the beginning to show this happens as a result of something else. Here it connects to the next word تَرَىٰنِى (see you), showing 'then I will see you'.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb
Added ت at beginning to show 'you' and the weak letter ي changes to ى at the end because it's in present tense

steps.m: you see me (in response to Moses asking to see Allah)
steps.n: Added نِى at the end to show that 'me' is the object being seen. In this ayah, this is Allah's response to Moses asking to see Him.
Starting with the basic time particle لم and adding shadda for emphasis
Adding ا at the end makes it specifically connect two events that happened in the past

steps.m: and then when/so when (connecting تَجَلَّىٰ with what came before)
steps.n: Adding فَ at the beginning shows this event follows from what happened before. Here it connects to when Allah manifested His glory (تَجَلَّىٰ).
Added the shadda (ّ) to show extra strength in the meaning
Added تَ at the beginning to show the action is happening to itself
Added ىٰ at the end to show this is a past tense verb meaning 'revealed himself'. This describes when Allah showed Himself to the mountain.

steps.m: to care for and help grow
steps.n: The doubled ب shows intensity, making it mean 'lord' or 'master'

steps.m: one who cares for and helps grow
steps.n: Connected the letters and doubled the middle letter to show intensity of meaning

steps.m: the one who cares for and helps grow (subject of كَلَّمَ)
steps.n: Added damma to show this is the doer of the action in كَلَّمَ (spoke to)

steps.m: his Lord (the one who spoke to him)
steps.n: Added هُۥ (meaning 'his') at the end to show this is his Lord. This word is doing the action of speaking to Moses.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a proper word
Added ال to make it specific
Added لِ (li) at the start to mean 'to/towards', like pointing to the mountain

steps.m: to make, set, or place something
steps.n: This shows the basic meaning of putting or making something into a certain state
Added vowel marks to show this is a past tense verb done by 'he'

steps.m: he made it (referring to the mountain that Allah manifested Himself to)
steps.n: Added the pronoun 'him/it' at the end to show what was affected by the action. In this ayah, it refers to the mountain (الْجَبَلِ) that was made into rubble.
Added basic vowels to connect the letters and make it a past tense verb
The doubled ك shows intensity in the crushing action

steps.m: crushed into complete powder (describing how Allah made the mountain crumble)
steps.n: The added ًا at the end shows HOW the mountain was crushed - completely and totally into powder

steps.m: to fall down suddenly or heavily

steps.n: Added fatha vowels to create basic past tense verb
Combined the repeated ر with a shadda to show intensity of the falling
Added و at beginning to connect this action with what happened before in the verse

steps.m: Moses (the one whom Allah spoke to directly)
steps.n: This is a proper noun borrowed from Hebrew/Egyptian meaning 'drawn out of water', referring to how Moses was found in the river as a baby. In this ayah, Moses is speaking directly with Allah.
Root letters got vowels to make past tense verb meaning 'he fainted/became unconscious'
Changed from a past tense verb (he fainted) to a describing word with tanween to show Musa's state
This is the basic temporal particle meaning 'when' that introduces a past event
Added فَ at the beginning to show this happens right after something else
The alif is elongated (آ) for emphasis and to mark the end of the word clearly when reading
Connected the root letters with vowels. The middle letter و changes to ا which is common with hollow verbs (verbs with و or ي in the middle).

steps.m: he regained consciousness (after being unconscious from seeing Allah's manifestation)
steps.n: Adding أَ at the beginning shows this is about becoming conscious again after being in an unconscious state. In this ayah, Musa regained consciousness after falling unconscious from seeing Allah's manifestation on the mountain.
Added short vowels (fatha) to make the past tense verb meaning 'he said'
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a weak letter between two fathas. This word is responding to when Moses said رَبِّ (my Lord).

steps.m: to swim, float, glide freely (and by extension: to be free from faults/to glorify)

steps.n: The word changed from a basic verb meaning 'to be free/glorify' into a special noun form that means 'complete/perfect glorification' - like saying 'super-glorified!'

steps.m: Glory be to You! (object of قَالَ - he said)
steps.n: Attached pronoun كَ makes it directed to Allah. This is what Musa said after seeing Allah's manifestation on the mountain.
The middle letter و drops out in this form, and we add ُ on first letter and ْ on second letter to make the basic command form
Added تُ at the end to show 'I' did this action in the past. This word is part of Musa's statement to Allah after he recovered from fainting.
Added sukun before adding pronoun suffix. This is a preposition that shows direction or destination.

steps.m: towards you (referring to Allah in أَنظُرْ إِلَيْكَ - let me look at You)
steps.n: Added ك at the end which means 'you' when speaking to one male. In this ayah, Musa (Moses) is speaking directly to Allah.
This is a personal pronoun meaning 'I'. The fatha marks help with pronunciation.
Added و with fatha at the beginning to mean 'and'
Added a small mark (۠) above the final alif to show this is being said in direct speech to Allah

steps.m: to be first or come before something

steps.n: Root letters connected with short 'a' vowels (fatha) to make the word 'first'
Doubled the middle letter to make the meaning stronger

steps.m: the foremost one (of الْمُؤْمِنِينَ - the believers)
steps.n: Added ُ at the end to show this is the doer in the sentence. Here it's describing Musa (Moses) as being the foremost of the believers.
Added مُ at start and vowels to show this describes someone who does the action
Added ين to show multiple believers. This word follows أَوَّلُ (first) in the ayah, showing Musa saying he is 'first of the believers'
Added ٱلْ to specify these particular believers that Musa is referring to

steps.m: he said (introducing what Allah says to موسى)
steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to show this happened in the past. This word introduces Allah's direct speech to Moses.
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in Arabic verbs with و in the middle
Added vowel marks to show how to pronounce the name correctly

steps.m: O Moses! (being directly addressed by اللَّهُ)
steps.n: Added يٰ at the beginning to show we're calling out to Moses directly. This is like saying 'Hey Moses!' or 'O Moses!' in English. In this ayah, Allah is directly addressing Moses.
This is the basic emphatic particle that adds certainty to what follows. The نّ has a shadda to show emphasis.
Adding ى at the end shows this is about 'me' (first person). In this ayah, Allah is speaking to Moses, saying 'Indeed I have chosen you.'
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Added اِ at start and changed ت to ط after ص to show choosing/selecting something specially for oneself
Added تُ to show 'I' did the action. The final ى changes to يْ before تُ
Added كَ to show 'you' as the object being chosen. The initial اِ changes to ٱ in continuous speech. This word follows إِنِّى (indeed I) in the ayah.
This is a preposition that shows something is on top of, over, or upon something else. In this ayah, it connects to 'the people' (ٱلنَّاسِ) to show Allah chose Musa over/above all other people.

steps.m: to be social or familiar with others

steps.n: Changed the root into a common noun meaning 'people' by adding a long 'aa' sound
Adding the definite article ال makes the noun definite
Adding shadda doubles the ن to emphasize the word
The kasra (ِ) at the end shows this word comes after عَلَى (meaning 'above/over') in the sentence
Changed the root 'to send' into a noun 'message' by adding an alif and vowels
Adding ة makes it mean 'one specific message' instead of messages in general
Regular singular word 'message' becomes plural by adding ات and changing the last alif
Added preposition ب meaning 'with/by'
Added ى at end to show possession (my). This word is receiving the action of اصْطَفَيْتُكَ (I have chosen you)
Changes the verb into a noun meaning 'speech' or 'words'
Adds 'and' at the beginning to connect this with the previous phrase about 'messages' (بِرِسٰلٰتِى)
Adds 'by/with' to show how Allah chose Moses - through His messages and words
The ending gets a kasra (i-sound) because it's connected to the word after it
Adds 'my' to show these are Allah's words to Moses. The alif is written as ٰ when followed by ى

steps.m: to take or grasp something with your hand

steps.n: Changed to command form by: 1) removing the أ at start, 2) adding ُ on first letter, and 3) adding ْ on second letter. This tells one person to do the action, like saying 'Take it!'

steps.m: so take! (responding to اصْطَفَيْتُكَ - I have chosen you)
steps.n: Added فَ at start to show this is a response to being chosen. It's like saying 'Since I chose you, then take what I give you'
This is a relative pronoun meaning 'what'. In this ayah, it refers to what Allah gave to Musa, making it the object of the verb ءَاتَيْتُكَ (I gave you).
The alif is written with a special elongated form (آ) to show it's pronounced longer. This is just a writing style and doesn't change the meaning.
Changed from basic 'come' to 'made something come/gave' by adding long alif at start. Past tense 'he' form ends with ى because it's a special type of verb.
Changed from 'he gave' to 'I gave' by adding تُ at the end. The ى changes to يْ when adding تُ.

steps.m: I gave you (referring to Moses in this ayah)
steps.n: Added كَ suffix to show who received the action. In this ayah, Allah is speaking to Moses.
Changed to a simple command meaning 'Be!' The middle letter و is dropped to make it shorter and easier to say as a command.

steps.m: and be! (command to Moses to be grateful)
steps.n: Added و to connect this command with the previous phrase where Allah tells Moses He has chosen him. The و shows this is an additional instruction.
This is the basic form of the word that means 'from'. It's used to show where something starts or comes from.
The shadda (ّ) appears because this word comes after a word ending in ن or tanween, causing the two ن sounds to merge together
The fatha (َ) at the end shows this word connects to the word after it (الشَّاكِرِينَ meaning 'the thankful ones')
Added vowels to connect the root letters together
This pattern shows who does the action, like changing 'thank' to 'thankful person'
Makes the word plural and shows it comes after the word مِنَ (from/among)
Adding ٱل makes it specific and doubles the ش letter
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Added نا at the end to show 'we' did the action instead of 'he'

steps.m: and we wrote (connected to what came before)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this action to the previous one
This is a preposition showing direction or purpose, like saying something is 'for' or going 'to' someone

steps.m: for him (connected to كَتَبْنَا - We wrote)
steps.n: Added هُ meaning 'him' and the elongation mark ۥ which helps with pronunciation. This shows who the writing was for, connecting back to the verb كَتَبْنَا (We wrote).
Basic form of the preposition that shows location or containment. In this ayah, it shows that something was written in/on the tablets.
The ي changes to ى when it comes at the end of a word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.
Root meaning 'shine' becomes a noun for 'tablet' by adding vowels - like how a tablet shines/shows writing
Changed to broken plural form - a special Arabic plural that changes the word's pattern
Added ال to make it definite and ِ because it comes after the word فِى (in)

steps.m: from (connects كُلِّ شَىْءٍ to what was written)
steps.n: This is a preposition that connects what follows it (كُلِّ شَىْءٍ - every matter) to what came before. In this ayah, it shows that Allah wrote something from every matter on the tablets.
The doubled ل with damma makes it mean 'all' or 'every'
The kasra at the end shows this word is being used after a preposition or as a possessive

steps.m: to be, to exist, to become

steps.n: Root letters combine to make the common noun meaning 'a thing'
Added tanwin kasra at the end to show this word is owned by كُلِّ (every/all). The ي is written as ى which is just a spelling variation.

steps.m: to advise or warn with wisdom

steps.n: Changed from action to the result of that action (like changing 'teach' to 'lesson')
The doubled م makes the meaning stronger, like saying 'very serious advice'

steps.m: a powerful advice/warning (that teaches wisdom) (object of كَتَبْنَا - we wrote)
steps.n: The ً (tanween) at the end shows this is the object - it's what was written/given (like saying 'an advice' in English)
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'detailed explanation'. This word is one of the things that Allah wrote (كَتَبْنَا) on the tablets.
Added ًا at the end to show this is a direct object (something being written down in detail)

steps.m: and a detailed explanation (coordinated with مَّوْعِظَةً)
steps.n: Added وَ to connect this with مَّوْعِظَةً (admonition), showing these are two things Allah wrote on the tablets
The doubled middle letter makes the meaning stronger and more complete
Added لِ at the beginning to show purpose or direction
Added shadda on the ل to emphasize the meaning
Basic root meaning
Added fatha on first letter and sukun on second letter to make it into a noun meaning 'thing'
The hamza (أ) changes to (ء) at the end of the word according to spelling rules
The word gets two dots under it at the end to show it's being used after a preposition
No further changes needed - the word is complete with its genitive case ending
Basic root meaning
Changed into a command by dropping the first letter and adding ُ and ْ marks. This tells one person to do something.
Added فَ at the beginning to show this command follows from what was mentioned before (the giving of the tablets)

steps.m: so take them! (referring to the tablets)
steps.n: Added هَا at the end to show what is being taken (the tablets mentioned in the previous verse)
Basic root meaning
Changes from verb 'to be strong' into the noun 'strength'

steps.m: with strength (related to فَخُذْهَا - so take it)
steps.n: Added بِ at beginning to show 'with' and tanween at end to show indefiniteness. This describes how to take (فَخُذْهَا) the tablets mentioned earlier.
Basic root meaning
Changed the word into a command form by adding sukun (ْ) on first letter, damma (ُ) on middle letter, and sukun (ْ) at the end. This makes it tell one person to do something.

steps.m: and command! (to one person) (connected to فَخُذْهَا - so take it)
steps.n: Added و with fatha (َ) at the beginning to connect this command with the previous command 'take it' (فَخُذْهَا) in the ayah

steps.m: to stand firm, rise up, establish
steps.n: Basic root meaning
The root ق-و-م became the noun قَوْمَ (people/folk) and got a fatha at the end because it's receiving the action of the command verb (someone is commanding the people)
Adds ك (your) to show these are 'your people'. The word is the object - the ones being commanded.
Basic root meaning
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start, sukun after first letter, and damma on last root letter
Added وا to show multiple people doing the action
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Added أ at start and changed vowels to make comparative/superlative form
Added بِ at start to show 'with/by'

steps.m: with its best (referring to the tablets/commandments)
steps.n: Added هَا to show possession by the tablets (feminine plural الألواح mentioned earlier in the verse)
Basic root meaning
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changed to Form IV pattern to make the meaning causative (making someone else see = showing)
Added 'sa' at start to make it 'I will' instead of 'was'
Added second person masculine plural object pronoun
Basic root meaning
Added vowel marks (fatha) to make it mean 'he dwelled' in the past tense
The weak letter و changes to ا when we add an alif after the first letter
Basic root meaning
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past

steps.m: one who breaks away from obedience
steps.n: Changes from describing an action to describing someone who does that action

steps.m: those who break away from obedience
steps.n: Makes the word plural to talk about multiple people

steps.m: the ones who break away from obedience
steps.n: Makes it specific which disobedient ones we're talking about

steps.m: the ones who break away from obedience
steps.n: The fatha at the end helps show this word is being used as an object in the sentence
Basic meaning of the root letters
Adding vowel marks to create basic past tense verb
Adding أ at beginning shows 'I' am doing the action, changing vowels for present tense
Adding س at beginning shows this will happen in the future
This is a preposition that shows separation or distance. In this ayah, it shows that people are being turned away from Allah's signs (ءَايٰتِىَ).
Forms the singular noun for a clear sign or miracle. The hamza and yaa combine into a long alif.
Forms the plural by replacing ة with ات

steps.m: My signs/verses (object of سَأَصْرِفُ - I will turn away from)
steps.n: Adds first person possessive suffix and changes spelling to show proper pronunciation. This word is what Allah will turn away from in the phrase 'I will turn away from My signs'
Starting with the demonstrative base that points to something
Changed from 'this/that' to 'who/which' by adding the dot under (kasra) sound
Added ال to make it definite and doubled the ذ with shadda

steps.m: those who (subject of يَتَكَبَّرُونَ - they are arrogant)
steps.n: Changed to plural form by adding ين and changed initial alif to wasl form. This word refers to the arrogant people mentioned later in the verse.
Added ت at start and doubled ب to show someone making themselves act big/great
Changed from basic command form to 'he' form in present tense by adding يَ at start and ُ at end, showing ongoing arrogant action
Plural suffix ون changes subject from 'he' to 'they'
This is a basic preposition showing location. In this ayah, it shows that something happens 'in the earth'.
The ي at the end is written as ى when it comes at the end of a word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.
These separated letters show the basic idea of earth/ground
The root letters are given vowels (fatha and sukun) to make the word 'earth' that we can use in a sentence
Adding ال at the beginning makes it specific - 'the earth' instead of just any earth

steps.m: of/in/on the earth (connected to يَتَكَبَّرُونَ فِى)
steps.n: Adding kasra at the end shows this word comes after فِى (in/on) in the phrase يَتَكَبَّرُونَ فِى (they are arrogant in)
Basic noun form showing something that is different or other than something else
Added kasra at the end to show connection to the next word الْحَقِّ (the truth)
Added بِ at beginning to show 'by means of' or 'with'
The doubled letter (ّ) makes it mean the actual thing (truth) instead of the action (being truthful)
Adding ال makes it point to a specific truth, not just any truth

steps.m: of the truth (connected to بِغَيْرِ meaning 'without')
steps.n: The ِ at the end shows this word comes after بِغَيْرِ (without), making it mean 'without the truth'

steps.m: if/whether (introduces a condition about يَرَوْا۟ 'they see')
steps.n: This is a conditional particle that introduces hypothetical situations. Here it starts a condition about 'if they see' something, followed by how they would react.

steps.m: and if (connects to previous similar conditions)
steps.n: Adds 'and' to connect this condition with the previous similar conditions about what they might see and how they would react

steps.m: to see or understand with eyes or mind

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added يَ at the start to make it present tense (like 'sees' instead of 'saw'). The middle and end letters merge into ى because they flow together when spoken

steps.m: they see (subject of يَتَّخِذُوهُ - they take it)
steps.n: Added وْا۟ at the end to show multiple people are doing the action. This word is connected to يَتَّخِذُوهُ (they take it) in the ayah, showing what the same group sees and then does.
The doubled ل with ُ on first letter makes it mean 'every' or 'all'
Added َ at the end because this word is the object of يَرَوْا (they see) in this ayah

steps.m: to show, make clear, or be evident

steps.n: Adding ة at the end turns this into a feminine noun meaning 'sign'
Adding tanween kasra (two dots below) shows this is indefinite and in genitive case
The word is the same - just written in a different way. The dots under show it's being acted upon
This is the basic negative particle that makes the following verb يُؤْمِنُوا۟ (they believe) into 'they do not believe'
The shadda (ّ) makes the negation stronger, emphasizing that they absolutely will not believe. This emphasis comes from combining the ل at the beginning with the لا, making the ل doubled.

steps.m: to cause to feel secure/to believe
steps.n: Adding long alif at start changes meaning to 'make someone do the action'
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels
Added plural suffix وا to show multiple people doing the action
Starting with the basic preposition that shows how something is done
Added the feminine pronoun هَا to show this refers back to the sign (آيَة) mentioned earlier in the verse
Combines و (and) with إن (if) to form a conditional phrase
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to form the basic past tense verb
Added يَ at start to make it present tense. The middle أ and final ي merge into one letter ى (called alif maqsurah)

steps.m: they see/perceive (in the context of those who are arrogant)
steps.n: Added وْا to show multiple people doing the action. The small circle (۟) is added to mark the plural ending. This refers to the arrogant people mentioned earlier in the verse.
Connected the root letters and added vowels to create the basic noun meaning path/way
Added fatha at the end to show this is the object of the verb يَرَوْا۟ (they see)

steps.m: to guide or follow the right way
steps.n: Basic meaning of following or showing the correct path
Changed from a basic root meaning 'guide' into a noun meaning 'guidance' by adding the vowels u and silence marks
Added 'al' to make it specific/definite, like changing from 'guidance' to 'the guidance'

steps.m: of the right guidance (object of سَبِيلَ/path)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end because it follows the word 'path' (سَبِيلَ) in the ayah
Basic negative particle that negates the verb يَتَّخِذُوهُ (they take it) in this ayah. It's like adding 'not' before a verb in English.
Added fatha mark (َ) to help with pronunciation. This doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: to grasp or take hold of
steps.n: Basic root meaning to take
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Added Form VIII pattern showing the action is done for oneself
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Changed from 'he' to 'they' by adding وا at the end
Added هُ to show what is being taken (referring to سَبِيلَ ٱلْغَىِّ - the path of error mentioned in the ayah)
Root meaning related to path or way
Adding ي and vowels creates the common noun meaning 'path'

steps.m: a path/way (object of يَتَّخِذُوهُ - they take it as)
steps.n: Added tanween fatHa to show this is the object being taken as a path. This connects to the verb يَتَّخِذُوهُ (they take it as) in the ayah.
This is a compound particle made of 'wa' (and) + 'in' (if)
These three letters together represent the basic concept of seeing
Added vowels (fatha) to make it past tense 'he saw'
Added يَ at start to show present tense. The root letters أ and ي merge into a long ى at the end
Changed from 'he sees' to 'they see' by adding و for plural
Added ا۟ at the end to make it a plural verb 'they see'. This word connects to 'they do not believe' later in the ayah.
Adding basic vowels to make the word readable
Adding ي makes it mean 'path' - a common pattern in Arabic for paths/ways
Added fatha at the end to show this is the object of the verb يَرَوْا (they see)

steps.m: to wander off the right path
steps.n: This root is about straying from the right path
Root letters connected with vowels to make past tense 'he' form
The و changes to ي and gets doubled with shadda to show how serious the straying is
Adding ال makes it point to a specific instance of straying

steps.m: of the serious straying (possessed by سَبِيلَ - path)
steps.n: The kasra at the end shows this word belongs to the word 'path' before it, making the phrase 'path of straying'
This is the root meaning showing the basic concept
Connected the letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Added يَ at start to show present tense, changed vowels to show ongoing action
Added ت with shadda and changed vowels to show reflexive meaning (doing action to oneself)
Added و to change from 'he' to 'they'
Added هُ at the end to show what is being taken

steps.m: to travel or follow a path

steps.n: Adding ي and vowels changes it from a basic root into a noun meaning 'path'

steps.m: a path/way (object of يَتَّخِذُوهُ - they take)
steps.n: Added tanween alif to show this is the object being taken in the phrase 'they take it as a path'
The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) to show distance
The ل with kasra (لِ) is added to emphasize distance
The كَ is added at the end to show we're pointing to something specific that was just mentioned
This is an emphatic particle that adds certainty to what follows
Adding بِ at the beginning changes the meaning to show cause or reason
Adding هُمْ shows who the action relates to - in this case, those who were rejecting Allah's signs

steps.m: to deny, to reject as false

steps.n: Doubling the middle letter ذ shows the denial was strong and definite. This word relates to how they responded to سَبِيلَ ٱلرُّشْدِ (the path of guidance) mentioned earlier in the ayah.
Plural marker وا changes subject from singular to plural 'they'
Forms the singular noun for 'sign' by lengthening the first vowel and adding ة
Replace singular ة with plural ات ending. This word is the object (what is being turned away from) in the sentence where Allah says 'I will turn away...'
Add preposition بِ to indicate 'with' or 'in'

steps.m: with Our signs (object of كَذَّبُوا - they denied)
steps.n: Adds 'نا' at the end to make it 'our signs' instead of just 'signs'. Connected to the word كَذَّبُوا (they denied) in the verse.
This root represents the basic concept of existing or being in a state
Added fathas to make past tense. The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a weak letter between two fathas.
Added وا with damma before it to change from 'he' to 'they' (masculine plural)

steps.m: and they were (disbelievers mentioned in previous ayah)
steps.n: Added وَ at beginning to connect this phrase to the previous statement about the disbelievers
This is a particle that shows something is moving or turning away from something else
Added ها which refers back to the signs (آيات) mentioned earlier that the people are being turned away from. This connects to غٰفِلِينَ (heedless) showing what they were heedless of.
Added alif after first letter and vowels to show who does the action
Added ين at the end to show this refers to many people
Changed regular alif to alif with overbar and added fatha at end since this word describes who/what 'they were'
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun that points to someone or something specific
Adding ن at the end makes it refer to multiple people instead of just one person
Adding و at the start connects this to what came before. This word is the subject (doer) of كَذَّبُوا (they denied) in this ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Adding shadda doubles the middle letter to show intensity
Added وا to show multiple people did the action. This word is connected to حَبِطَتْ (became worthless) in the ayah, showing whose deeds became worthless.

steps.m: to show, make clear, or be apparent

steps.n: Root letters combine to make the word for 'sign', with a long 'aa' sound at start
Changes the singular to plural form
Adds the preposition 'with/by' to show how something was done

steps.m: with Our signs (object of كَذَّبُوا - they denied)
steps.n: Adds 'our' to show these are Allah's signs. This word is being denied/rejected by the disbelievers in this verse.

steps.m: to meet, encounter, or come face to face with

steps.n: Changed the verb into a noun by adding a long ا and changing ي to ء. This makes it mean the action itself instead of doing the action.
Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to the verb كَذَّبُوا (they denied), meaning they denied the meeting/encounter.
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it in the verse.

steps.m: to be behind or come after something

steps.n: Changed root letters to make 'the one who/that comes after' - like saying 'later' or 'last'

steps.m: the afterlife (what comes after this life)
steps.n: Added ة to turn the word from 'later/after' into 'the afterlife' - like turning a description into a specific thing
Added ال (the) at beginning and kasra at end to show it's owned by the word لِقَآءِ (meeting of)
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past

steps.m: it (feminine) became void/worthless (referring to أَعْمٰلُهُمْ - their deeds)
steps.n: Added feminine past tense marker ت to match with أَعْمٰلُهُمْ (their deeds) which is feminine
Root letters got vowels to make it mean 'one action' instead of 'to act'
Changes from single deed to multiple deeds by following a common plural pattern
Changed alif to alif maqsurah for writing style
Added damma to show this is the subject

steps.m: their deeds (subject of حَبِطَتْ - became worthless)
steps.n: Added possessive pronoun suffix meaning 'their'. This word is the subject of حَبِطَتْ (became worthless) in this ayah.
This is a simple word that turns a statement into a yes/no question, like adding a question mark in English. The َ (fatha) and ْ (sukun) marks help show how to pronounce it correctly.

steps.m: to give what is due (reward or punishment)

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the past tense verb. The final ي changes to ى because it's a weak letter at the end.

steps.m: he is given what is due
steps.n: Added يُ at start to show present tense and changed vowels to make it passive (showing someone receives the action instead of does it)

steps.m: they are given what is due
steps.n: Added وْنَ at the end to show multiple people are receiving the action. The ى changes to و because it's a weak letter before نَ.
This is a basic particle used for exception. The shadda (ّ) emphasizes the 'l' sound to show strong exception.
Adding ا at the end makes this word connect better with what follows it in a sentence. In this ayah, it's connecting to show that people will only be rewarded based on what they used to do.
This is a relative pronoun that connects to the verb يَعْمَلُونَ (they used to do) in this ayah. It helps ask about or refer to actions that were done.
Added vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changed to common past tense form with weak middle letter
Added وا at the end to change 'he' to 'they'. This word is the subject of يَعْمَلُونَ (they do/work) in this ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make past tense verb
Added يَ at start and changed vowels to show ongoing action
Added ون at the end to show multiple people doing the action
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
The ت combines with first letter to show doing something with effort/purpose
Added و (wa) at the beginning to mean 'and'
Connected the root letters with fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to create the noun form meaning 'people'
Added damma at the end to show this is the subject doing the action in the sentence
This is a proper name borrowed from Hebrew. It doesn't follow Arabic root patterns and has no morphological derivation in Arabic.
Added damma (ُ) on م and fatha (َ) on س to show correct pronunciation
Added alif khanjariyah (ٰ) which is a small alif written above the ى to indicate a long 'aa' sound

steps.m: from (connects حُلِيِّهِمْ to what comes before)
steps.n: This is a preposition that shows where something comes from or what it's made of. In this ayah, it connects 'their jewelry' (حُلِيِّهِمْ) to what happened before, showing what material was used.

steps.m: from (connects حُلِيِّهِمْ to what comes before)
steps.n: Added a small ن (called nun ghunna) at the end to help with pronunciation when connecting to the next word. This doesn't change the meaning.
Added vowel marks (fatha and sukun) to the root letters to make a word meaning 'after'
Added kasra at the end to show this word is being possessed by something that comes after it
Added هِۦ at the end to show this means 'after him', referring to Moses who was mentioned earlier
This is a helper word that shows the calf was made from their jewelry. It connects the word حُلِيِّ (jewelry) with عِجْلًا (calf) in this ayah.
Changes to passive voice - something was decorated by someone else
Changed from a verb (was decorated) to a noun (jewelry) with extra emphasis
Added 'their' to show who owns the jewelry

Changed the root meaning of quick movement into a noun for a young cow (calf). The connection comes from how calves are known for their quick, energetic movements. This word is what the people of Moses made from their jewelry.

steps.m: a calf (object that was made by قَوْمُ مُوسَىٰ)
steps.n: The ًا ending shows this is indefinite (any calf) and is the object being acted upon - it's what Moses's people made from their jewelry.
Connected the root letters with fatha to create a noun meaning a physical form. This word describes the golden calf (عِجْلًا) that was made.

steps.m: a body, physical form (describing عِجْلًا - calf)
steps.n: Added tanween alif to show this describes how the calf appeared - as a physical form. This matches the ending of عِجْلًا which it describes.
Starting with the basic preposition that shows direction or possession
The letter gets doubled (shown by the shadda mark) to connect smoothly with the word before it

steps.m: for it/to it (referring to عِجْلًا - the calf)
steps.n: Added pronoun هُ to show what is being referred to (the calf mentioned earlier in the verse)

steps.m: for it/to it (referring to عِجْلًا - the calf)
steps.n: Added ۥ as a pronunciation helper mark to show the elongation of the final vowel

steps.m: to make a low sound (like a cow mooing)

steps.n: Changes the action into a noun for the sound itself. This word describes the calf (عِجْلًا) mentioned earlier in the verse.
Adds tanween (double damma) to show this is any mooing sound, not a specific one
This is a negative particle that changes present tense verbs to past tense negative meaning
Added أَ at the beginning to turn the negative statement into a question. This is asking about the verb يَرَوْا۟ (they saw) that comes after it.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added يَ at start to show present tense. The middle and last letters (أ and ي) combine into one long ى because they're weak letters that often merge together

Adding ي makes this a permanent path/way rather than temporary condition

Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it

steps.m: that they (the ones who saw they had gone astray)

Added fatha vowels to make it a past tense verb

steps.m: he forgives (in response to رَحِمَ - has mercy)

Adds -ah ending to make it mean 'one bad deed' instead of just 'bad'
Makes it plural to show multiple bad deeds
Adds 'the' to specify these particular bad deeds and adds kasra at end to show connection to what follows

steps.m: then (connects تَابُوا۟ with what came before)
steps.n: This word shows that repentance (تَابُوا۟) happened after doing bad deeds. It's like saying 'first they did bad things, then they repented'

steps.m: to turn back or return (especially to Allah in repentance)
he turned back (to Allah in repentance)
steps.n: Connected the root letters and added vowels to make a past tense verb. The middle letter و changes to ا because it has fathas before and after it.

steps.m: they turned back (to Allah in repentance) (subject of عَمِلُوا)
steps.n: Added وا at the end to show many people did this action. This matches with عَمِلُوا (they did) earlier in the verse, showing these are the same people.
This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. Here it connects to the word بَعْدِهَا (after it) to show timing.
Added a small ن mark (called a 'small noon') after the main ن. This helps show how to pronounce it smoothly with the next word.
Added vowel marks (fatHa and sukun) to the root letters to make the word mean 'after'
Added kasra to show this word is possessed by something that comes after it

steps.m: after it/them (referring to السيئات - the evil deeds)
steps.n: Added ها to show this refers back to السيئات (the evil deeds) mentioned earlier in the verse
Changed the word to mean actively choosing to believe, rather than just feeling trust
Added وا to show multiple people did the action
Added و at the beginning to connect this word to the previous action in the verse
Basic form of the word that introduces conditions or statements
Adding shadda (doubling mark) on ن changes this from a conditional word to one that adds emphasis to the statement that follows. Here it emphasizes that 'your Lord' (رَبَّكَ) is truly forgiving.

steps.m: one who nurtures completely (subject of إِنَّ)
steps.n: The doubled middle letter shows this is someone who nurtures completely and perfectly. This word is affected by إِنَّ (indeed/truly) which comes before it in the ayah.
Adding كَ at the end shows this belongs to you or relates to you. The word comes after إِنَّ (indeed/truly) in the ayah, which affects how it's pronounced.

This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it connects the verb 'they repented' (تَابُوا۟) with 'after it' (بَعْدِهَا) to show when the repentance happened.
Added a small ن (called nun ghunna) at the end because the next word starts with ب. This helps make the pronunciation smoother.

steps.m: to be far away from something

steps.n: Connected the letters with vowel marks to create a word meaning 'after' - this comes from the idea that what comes after is far away from what came before
Added kasra to show this word is possessed by something (the evil deeds mentioned earlier)
Added هَا which refers back to السَّيِّـَٔاتِ (the evil deeds) mentioned earlier in the ayah
Added vowels and و to show someone who forgives a lot
Added tanween to show this is a general description
Added لَ at the beginning to emphasize that this quality is certain
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a past tense verb
Added ي to show this is a strong/intense quality

steps.m: Most Merciful (as a name/attribute of Allah)
steps.n: Changes from -a ending to -un ending to show it's a name/title of Allah
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation. لَمَّا is used for events that happened in the past and connects them to what happens next.
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before. This shows that one event follows another - when Moses's anger subsided, something else happened.
Basic past tense form showing the anger stopped affecting Musa. This word connects to مُّوسَى (Musa) in the ayah through the word عَن (from/about).
This is a preposition that shows something (anger) moving away or separating from something else (Moses). In this ayah, it shows the anger departing from Moses.
This is the basic form of the proper name Moses (موسى). It's a non-Arabic name adapted into Arabic usage, so it doesn't follow standard Arabic word patterns or have an Arabic root.
Added vowel marks to show the correct pronunciation: damma (ُ) on م and fatha (َ) on س.
Added shadda (ّ) on م because it comes after the word عَن. When certain words end in ن and the next word starts with م, the ن sound merges into the م, making it stronger (shown by the shadda).
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Added tanween damma to make it an indefinite noun
Makes the noun definite and shows it's the subject of سَكَتَ (subsided/calmed down)

steps.m: to take or grasp something physically or mentally

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to show this is a past tense verb. This word comes after سَكَتَ (subsided) in the ayah, showing what happened after Moses's anger calmed down.
Added vowel marks to connect letters and form singular noun. The meaning relates to something that shows or displays things clearly.
Forms broken plural indicating multiple tablets, specifically referring to the tablets given to Prophet Musa
Made definite with ال and marked as direct object with fatha since it's the object of أَخَذَ (he took)
Basic preposition meaning 'in' - one of the most common Arabic prepositions
Added و at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it, like saying 'and' in English
Changed the spelling of ي to ى since it comes at the end of the word. This is just a writing change and doesn't affect the meaning.

steps.m: to write down, to copy, to record

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to form the basic past tense verb
Changed vowels to create a noun meaning the result of copying
Added ة to show one specific copy
Changed ending to show this copy belongs to something (the tablets)

steps.m: its copy (referring to the tablets الألواح)
steps.n: Added ها to show this is the tablets' copy/inscription
